issue_title: dmstodd gives wrong results when degrees has 3 digits
issue_body:
When the degrees of longitude reach 3 digits, the `dmstodd()` method gives incorrect results because the digits representing the minutes are extracted using a 2-character offset relative to the start of the string.

```
>>> import mgrs
>>> m = mgrs.MGRS()
>>> m.dmstodd("110000N")
11.0
>>> m.dmstodd("110000E")
11.0
>>> m.dmstodd("1110000E")
112.66666666666667
>>> m.dmstodd("1110000.00E")
112.66666666666667
```
This can be fixed by changing `[2:-2]` to `[-4:-2]` in two places in `dmstodd()` so that the two digits representing the minutes are extracted relative to the end of the string, not the beginning

issue_title: Cython check seems to break install via pip
Host value does not get escaped correctly
issue_body:
Describe the issue briefly here.

##### Steps to replicate

Values assigned to the host property don't get escaped correctly:

```python
>>> u = furl('https://user:pass@myhost.com/path/goes/here')
>>> u.host = 'evil:hahaha@haxx0r.com'
>>> str(u)
'https://user:pass@evil:hahaha@haxx0r.com/path/goes/here'
```

Trying to parse this value with furl again leads to an error now:

```python
>>> furl(str(u))
...
ValueError: Invalid port: 'hahaha@haxx0r.com'
```

##### Expected result

Either escape the value for the `host` property, or throw an error if this is impossible (i.e. even escaped chars are not allowed).

##### Actual result

A URL that's broken and ends up not being a valid URL.

issue_title: add redis cache
xmm register values not displayed (probable GDB 8.0 bug)
issue_body:
I'm on x86-64 Arch Linux (rolling release, updated last week), using python-gdbgui 0.7.9.5-1 built from https://aur.archlinux.org/packages/python-gdbgui/.

I have gdb 8.0 (from the Arch binary package), and my browser is Chromium 60.0.3112.101 (Developer Build) (64-bit) from the Arch binary package.  Linux kernel 4.12.8-2-ARCH.

----

I'm single-stepping through PLT lazy dynamic linker code that resolves `puts`, when called from `main` in a hello world program (just to see how well fetch disassembly mode works).  I compiled it with `gcc -O3 -g` (gcc7.1.1) for x86-64.

The "registers" dropdown has blank entries for xmm0-xmm15.  It shows zeros for ymm0-ymm15 (which is correct.)

gdb's own `tui` mode (`layout reg`  / `tui reg vector`) has a similar problem when I use gdb stand-alone: It shows just blank space for the ymm register values.  However, `p /x $xmm0.v4_int32` shows non-zero values in vanilla gdb and in gdbgui's console.

**I just realized this might all be a GDB bug, if the `mi2` command responses are bogus the same way the `tui` register pane is**.  Those could both be broken in gdb while console commands were unaffected.

gdbgui does correctly show the high lane, e.g. after `set $ymm5.v4_int64 = {0xffff, 0xffff, 0xffff, 0xffff}` I can see what I expect.  But `xmm5` is still blank.

MXCSR (the SSE control/status register) is sometimes changing to a non-zero value and then going back to `0` on instructions like `push` (which can't modify MSCSR).  The value displayed matches what I see from typing `p /x $mxcsr`, but that's clearly bogus because it means all exceptions are unmasked.  (A normal value for MXCSR is something like 0x1F80, which is the state when the machine boots.  See also https://software.intel.com/en-us/articles/x87-and-sse-floating-point-assists-in-ia-32-flush-to-zero-ftz-and-denormals-are-zero-daz).

However, I also see this in the `tui reg vector` pane.

Similarly, `fctrl`, `fstat`, and the other x87 status/control registers are spuriously changing when stepping over integer instructions.

I'm on a Skylake i7-6700k CPU, which supports MPX, so maybe gdb is getting confused by the presence of `bnd` registers?  `bnd0raw` 0-3 are also shown as blank in gdbgui.

These problems were all happening even before stepping over a `bnd` and `xgetbv` instruction in the dynamic-linker code, but maybe on a target that supports MPX, the layout of some struct changes to hold new registers, and that's the source of problems with both `tui reg` and the `--interpreter=mi2` interface?

issue_title: utf8 problem on Windows
issue_body:
In version 7.4.1., pylama cant open files containing utf-8 characters. When executed in --verbose mode, it points to file core.py:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "***\site-packages\pylama\core.py", line 42, in run
    with CodeContext(code, path) as ctx:
  File "***\site-packages\pylama\core.py", line 196, in __enter__
    self.code = self._file.read()
  File "***\encodings\cp1252.py", line 23, in decode
    return codecs.charmap_decode(input,self.errors,decoding_table)[0]
UnicodeDecodeError: 'charmap' codec can't decode byte 0x8d in position 835: character maps to <undefined>
```

This is caused by line 195 in core.py, because default encoding in Windows is not utf-8.
```
self._file = open(self.path, mode)
```

Changing the line to this fixes the issue.
```
self._file = open(self.path, mode, encoding='utf-8')
```

issue_title: Quantity objects not compatible with numpy testing assert_almost_equal
issue_body:
MWE:

```python
import numpy.testing as npt
from pint import UnitRegistry
Quantity = UnitRegistry().Quantity

x = Quantity(1.99999999, "ft")
y = Quantity(2, "ft")
npt.assert_almost_equal(x, y)
```
results in:
```
pint.unit.DimensionalityError: Cannot convert from 'foot' to 'dimensionless'
```

issue_title: strip_headers failure cases
issue_body:
Here's a list of files that have some degree of failure in strip_headers(): https://gist.github.com/ikarth/49e10e7b5a66fe8d6732

This was made by grepping over the English-language, public domain text files from the subset corpus I've been assembling (April 2010 DVD + another archive from 2013) for the first mention of "Project Gutenberg". 

There's a few false positives like "THE COMPLETE PROJECT GUTENBERG WORKS OF GEORGE MEREDITH" and a few transcriber notes, but the most common bits to stay untouched is the illustrated books that have a note about an html version with pictures being available. 

Removing or not removing some of these may be an aesthetic call, but I figured the data might be useful.

  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.4/dist-packages/homeassistant/components/sensor/openweathermap.py", line 181, in update
    obs = self.owm.weather_at_coords(self.latitude, self.longitude)
  File "/root/.homeassistant/deps/pyowm/webapi25/owm25.py", line 248, in weather_at_coords
    return self._parsers['observation'].parse_JSON(json_data)
  File "/root/.homeassistant/deps/pyowm/webapi25/observationparser.py", line 39, in parse_JSON
    d = loads(JSON_string)
  File "/usr/lib/python3.4/json/__init__.py", line 312, in loads
    s.__class__.__name__))
TypeError: the JSON object must be str, not 'NoneType'
```

I looked a little bit into it and this is effectively wrong : a call to JSON.load() is done on a NoneType object, which leads to a crash : 
https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/blob/master/pyowm/webapi25/observationparser.py#L39

The call comes from this line :
https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/blob/master/pyowm/webapi25/owm25.py#L248 and the method producing the None object is there 
https://github.com/csparpa/pyowm/blob/master/pyowm/commons/weather_client.py#L71

According to the documentation this method should return a string, and it's returning None in some cases.

I think a check is missing in _lookup_cache_or_invoke_API of the same file.

issue_title: Min-elements is not enforced for leaf-list if it's container's when statement is evaluated true
jsonxsl plugin -  type decimal64  is presented in quoted form in json
issue_body:
According to https://tools.ietf.org/html/draft-lhotka-netmod-yang-json-02 decimal64 type should be mapped to a JSON number. This mean it should appear in json in unquoted form. 
Using xsl generated by jsonxsl plugin results presenting such data in quotation.
It  can be easily fixed by patch:
--- a/pyang/plugins/jsonxsl.py
+++ b/pyang/plugins/jsonxsl.py
@@ -39,6 +39,7 @@ type_class = dict((t,"unquoted") for t in
                   ("boolean", "int8", "int16", "int32",
                    "uint8", "uint16", "uint32"))
 """Classification of types suited for JSON translation."""
+type_class.update(('decimal@' + str(t),"unquoted") for t in range(1,18))
 
 type_class.update((t,t) for t in
                   ("empty", "instance-identifier", "identityref", "string"))

and that is because parsing decimal type from yang results internal notation of such type: decimal64@<fraction-digits>, which leads to not finding it inside type_class dict.

issue_title: Ability to install mlxtend with conda
issue_body:
Hi,

I really love this package and the documentation, it's been very helpful for a machine learning beginner like myself. I am wondering if you would consider putting this package onto the Anaconda Cloud so that it can be downloaded with conda in addition to pip, like so:

```bash
conda install mlxtend
```

The benefit of doing this is it would make things easier for Windows users who install MiniConda (vs. Anaconda, which comes with a bunch of scientific computing packages by default) because some of this pacakge's dependencies (scipy, numpy, matplotlib) can be difficult to install on Windows via pip. 

Personally I am fine using pip, but I often share the code I write on Ubuntu with co-workers who use Windows, and for them using conda makes their lives easier. 

This is a very minor issue, but I hope it will make access to this package easier. 

Thanks!

issue_title: Bag distance calculation
issue_body:
Hello @chrislit,

First of all, I wanted to thank you for your tremendous work on this library. I have started not long ago a somewhat similar library for JavaScript called [Talisman](https://github.com/Yomguithereal/talisman) and stumbled upon your very well documented code quite early.

However, I started implementing the "bag" distance and the only article I found on this subject was this one: http://www-db.disi.unibo.it/research/papers/SPIRE02.pdf

> "String Matching with Metric Trees Using an Approximate Distance." Ilaria Bartolini, Paolo Ciaccia, and Marco Patella.

So I was wondering whether you had the same source because I may have found a mistake in your implementation.

In this [line](https://github.com/chrislit/abydos/blob/master/abydos/distance.py#L2423), your return the max of the differences using the python `len` function, which, on a `Counter` returns the number of distinct values in the set rather than the total number of items remaining. The article, or this how I understand it, seems to indicate we need to work on the total number of items in the difference, not the number of distinct items. This would be `len(set.elements())` rather than `len(set)`.

What do you think?

issue_title: Paramiko threads doesn't exit
issue_body:
Hi There.
When I'm running on SSH tunnel, on python 2.7.14 in windows, my Paramiko threads aren't existing and keep running forever, at the end of the program. 

in example:
```
from sshtunnel import SSHTunnelForwarder
from sqlalchemy import create_engine

ssh_server = SSHTunnelForwarder(ssh_address_or_host=('address', int(22)),
                                ssh_username='usr',
                                ssh_pkey='/sshkey/path',

                                remote_bind_address=('127.0.0.1', 3306),

engine.connect()
for i in range(1, 5):

issue_title: util.boundaries_to_intervals with labels is weird
issue_body: When `labels=None` (the default), `None` is returned.  We don't have any tests or code anywhere that doesn't use `labels=None`.  When `labels != None`, `labels[-1]` is returned.  There's no test that `len(labels) == len(boundaries)`, so this can lead to weird behavior.  It also pre-supposes that the final label should be discarded.  Is this actually a convention?  Is there any utility to cutting off the last label for the user?  Depending on the answer to these questions, I'm in favor of removing the `label` argument from `util.boundaries_to_intervals`.

issue_title: Python crashes after jedi imports similar C modules in one Python module
issue_body:
The steps to find this issue is firstly reported [here.](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/817)

In summary, [vscode-python](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python) uses an external Python process and jedi to provide, e.g., function parameter information of Python source files. However, when vscode-python submits to jedi a Python module file (.py) which contains two or more *similar* C modules ```import```s, jedi will import all C modules and then crashes the external Python process. Here is an example file having such issue: [_import_c_extension.py](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/python/_import_c_extension.py)

When vscode-python submits this file *_import_c_extension.py* to jedi, jedi imports both ```from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu import *``` and ```from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state import *``` to the external Python process. However, the C module ```caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu``` has similar objects (e.g., functions) as ```caffe2_pybind11_state```. They differ only in C function implementations: one for CPU only machine, and another for GPU-capable machine. Importing both C modules crashes the external Python process. Actually, I can reproduce the Python crash directly in terminal:
```
[myh@dsrl2 detectron]$ python2
Python 2.7.5 (default, Aug  4 2017, 00:39:18) 
[GCC 4.8.5 20150623 (Red Hat 4.8.5-16)] on linux2
Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state import *
>>> from caffe2.python.caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu import *
Key already registered.
Offending key: Python.
(crash)
[myh@dsrl2 detectron]$ 
```
This should be not a bug of [_import_c_extension.py](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2/blob/master/caffe2/python/_import_c_extension.py), because it conditionally imports only one of ```caffe2_pybind11_state_gpu``` and ```caffe2_pybind11_state```. vscode-python developers think the crash is an issue of jedi. See the [discussion.](https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/817) Isn't it?

Additionally, for reproducing this bug, one might need to build [caffe2](https://github.com/caffe2/caffe2) (with GPU enabled) (commit: 5651c4ce0279c895cbb1d1eeecc6594cc8791103) with GPU support (requiring a CUDA-capable graphics card) from source. Or writing a minimal set of C modules...

import jedi
filename = jedi.evaluate.imports.__file__

issue_title: On windows memory_maps() does not return anonymous maps
issue_body:

issue_title: Run using docker
issue_body:
Hi :wave: 

Seems like an interesting project. But I don't like polluting my system with global python deps.

So I'm documenting the steps to build this in docker for myself and others who may be interested.

## Setup

1) `cd` to `rtv` folder
2) `Dockerfile`: (includes `less` as it's a dependency for the `help` command)

```dockerfile
FROM python:3

RUN apt-get update && apt-get install less -y && pip install rtv

CMD [ "rtv" ]

```

3) `docker build -t rtv .`


## Running 
```bash
docker run -it rtv
```

## Issues

* Figure out auth. Currently, `rtv` requires a browser for OAuth, which doesn't seem possible to do safely with docker.

issue_title: Fix registration of NumPy dtypes
issue_body:
In numpy-1.14, dtype field lookup has been changed to be  by position/offset rather than by field name.  If that version is installed, an `ImportError` gets raised when importing `fwdpy11` because dtype fields are declared in a different order than how they appear in the structs they reflect.

Another user of `pybind11` has run into this: pybind/pybind11#1274.

This should be fixed ASAP and a new release of 0.13 should follow.

cc @jeromekelleher

Eager creation of asyncio.Lock requires an event loop.
issue_body:

issue_title: gradev broken, especially for frequency data
issue_body:
The gap resistant ADEV functions have some issues that should be easy to fix and a more serious issue concerning frequency data.

The easy to fix issues:
- calc_gradev_phase() calls the function uncertainty_estimate() that doesn't exist.
- the parameters mj and stride are not converted to integers in calc_gradev_phase().

The more important issue is that gradev() doesn't work properly for frequency data. If the input data is an array with NaN values, the frequency2phase() function is called and it sets all phase values to NaN after the first NaN value occurs in the frequency array. That's a property of np.cumsum().  If you are unlucky and the first value is a NaN you lose all data in this step. There is a function called trim_data() that trims NaNs from the beginning of an array, but it is not used anymore (since the API change).

Additionally, since the API change, the gradev example uses data_type='freq' and the example doesn't work very well due to the issue discussed above.

The core issue here is that for frequency data with gaps, the frequency data can not be converted to phase data and the gradev must be calculated based on the equations for the adev for fractional frequency data. 
At the moment all *dev calculations in allantools are based on phase data. What do you think about *dev calculations based on frequency data? 

As discussed in I. Sesia and P. Tavella, Metrologia, **45** (2008) p. 134, using the phase data for the adev calculation is preferred in the presence of data gaps, but sometimes there is no choice.

issue_title: [Windows] Running on Python 3.5/3.6 requires msvcp140.dll which apparently isn't shipped by Python
issue_body:
If you get such an error on Windows:
```Text
import cefpython3
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "C:\Python3\lib\site-packages\cefpython3\__init__.py", line 59, in <module>
    from . import cefpython_py36 as cefpython
ImportError: DLL load failed: The specified module could not be found.
```

Then most probably this is caused, because you are missing "msvcp140.dll" dependency (for Python 3 for example). This is a dependency for Python C++ extensions (eg. cefpython_py36.pyd depends on it). For Python 3 to fix this download [Visual C++ Redistributable for VS2015](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/download/details.aspx?id=52685) (13 MB) - this is just a VC++ redistributable, not a Visual Studio package. For 32-bit download "vc_redist.x86.exe" file and for 64-bit download "vc_redist.x64.exe" file.

Explanation: msvcp140.dll is the DLL for the C++ runtime library. This dependency is added by Cython when building CEF Python module. It seems that Python 3.6 only ships "vcruntime140.dll" which is the DLL for the C runtime library.

When using pyinstaller/py2exe tools for freezing application into exe then these tools should automatically detect the msvcp140.dll dependency and ship it with your application. So it's not an issue for packaged apps.

This info is also available in the Knowledge Base document.

## Possible solutions

1. Ship the msvcp140.dll file along with CEF and CEF Python binaries

issue_title: Passing constructor arguments to immutable base types
issue_body:
There's an oddity in the `RestrictedClass` type that will become a problem in Python 3. The constructor passes its arguments on to the base class constructor:

```python
class RestrictedClass(base_type):
  def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
    try:
      self.__check(args[0])
    except IndexError:
      pass
    super(RestrictedClass, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
```

If `base_type` is `int` or another immutable type, then in Python 3 this is rejected with:

    TypeError: object.__init__() takes no parameters

In Python 2 the inappropriate argument seems to be ignored.

IIUC, the way this should work for inheriting from immutable types is for the `__new__` method to initialise the data (as the `RestrictedClass.__new__` already does) and then not attempt to change it in `__init__`.

I see a couple of ways to solve this:

* Never pass arguments to the base class constructor; this works for simple types since `__new__` has already taken care of it, but may fail for `RestrictedClass` when inheriting from user-defined types.
* Check the base type against a list and don't pass on arguments for types known to be immutable. This doesn't feel very maintainable.
* Inspect the metadata of the base class constructor at run-time? Sounds horrendous.

A slightly more radical option would be not to use inheritance at all. Do we actually need inheritance here? It should be easy enough to build a class with composition that forwards on all methods to the contained value instance. The only thing I can see that we lose is the ability to use `isinstance()` on objects of this type, which is in any case not very Pythonic.

issue_title: Customize ajax error message when DEBUG = False
issue_body:
Hi, 

When an error occurs in production, we get, by default, an alert with the message "An error occurred while processing an AJAX request."

I know we can override the onError javascript function to display a custom message, but it would be helpful to be able to customize the default message. The current message is very "developer" oriented message, but not very user friendly (many users don't even know what an AJAX request is).

Besides, my sites are in French and Italian and I would like them to have by default a message in their own language.

I see 2 ways to achieve the above:
1) add a ugettext_lazy to the shortcuts.py file where the message is hard-coded. This way, we can always translate the sentence above to something completely different (more user friendly) even in English.
2) add a variable in the settings and use it instead of the hard-coded message. The hard-coded message would be used in the case the variable is not set in the settings file. The user would put the ugettext/ugettext_lazy is his settings directly or not according to his own needs. In this cause, we could create a dictionnary DJANGO_AJAX and add all the settings related to this app into it (like django-rest-framework does). This avoids having multiple variables across the settings.

I prefer the option 2, but am obviously open to discussion.

I'm willing to implement it and make a pull request if you agree.
What do you think?
Thanks

issue_title: Repetition opcode not respected in EPD boards
issue_body:
Per PGN reference: http://www.thechessdrum.net/PGN_Reference.txt
```
16.2.5.20: Opcode "rc": repetition count

The "rc" opcode is used to indicate the number of occurrences of the indicated
position.  It takes a single, positive integer operand.  Any position,
including the initial starting position, is considered to have an "rc" value of
at least one.  A value of three indicates a candidate for a draw claim by the
position repetition rule.
```
It appears that this opcode is accepted by python-chess, but the created board does not report as expected with `board.can_claim_threefold_repetition()`:
```python
>>> import chess
>>> board = chess.Board()
>>> board.set_epd('3r2k1/p2r2p1/1p1B2Pp/4PQ1P/2b1p3/P3P3/7K/8 w - - bm e6 ; ce 132; acs1; acn 122623; rc 5')
{'bm': [Move.from_uci('e5e6')], 'ce': 132, 'acs1': None, 'acn': 122623, 'rc': 5}
>>> board.can_claim_threefold_repetition()
False
>>>
```
Looking at the `set_epd()` code in python-chess, it seems the only supported opcodes are "hmvc" and "fmvn"[[1](https://github.com/niklasf/python-chess/blob/master/chess/__init__.py#L2391-L2394)], because the given EPD is translated into a FEN, which is then passed to `set_fen()` to actually create and return a Board. That means that `set_epd()` in principle can't support any part of the EPD specification that is not also supported by FEN, unless it is refactored to create its own Board without calling `set_fen()`. Additionally, there is no way to retrieve any of the given opcodes from the Board once it is created, as they are not stored anywhere.

`can_claim_threefold_repetition()` checks for threefold repetition by working through the move stack and counting transpositions.[[2](https://github.com/niklasf/python-chess/blob/master/chess/__init__.py#L1747-L1785)] But an EPD does not provide a move stack. There is no property of a chess.Board that corresponds to the EPD 'rc' opcode that I can find.

I'm not sure if this qualifies as a bug or a feature request. But it seems python-chess has an incomplete implementation of EPD.

issue_title: Validation of a dictionary runs in O(n^2)
issue_body:
Currently all keys in the dictionary are matched against all keys in the schema. If we have a large set of keys then the validation takes very long.

Here is a test that simulates the problem. A single validation should take under 1 ms but currently takes 12 ms.

```
def test_validation_performance():
    """
    Naive validation of a dictionary with many keys with a schema that has many keys
     This test verifies a single validation runs in under 1 msec
    :return:
    """
    number_if_keys_in_schema = 100
    iterations = 1000
    threshold_ms = 1

    schema_dict = {str(i): str for i in range(number_if_keys_in_schema)}

    schema = Schema(schema_dict)

    data = {str(i): str(i) for i in range(number_if_keys_in_schema)}

    start = time.time()

    for i in xrange(iterations):
        schema(data)

    total_elapsed = time.time() - start
    avg_per_round = round(total_elapsed * 1000 / iterations, 3)
    print("Total: %s secs, avg: %s ms" % (round(total_elapsed, 3), avg_per_round))

    assert avg_per_round < threshold_ms, "Avg validation %s > %s ms" % (avg_per_round, threshold_ms)
```

issue_title: Wrong folder scanned for group/host_vars
issue_body:
At these lines https://github.com/fboender/ansible-cmdb/blob/master/src/ansiblecmdb/ansible.py#L126 and https://github.com/fboender/ansible-cmdb/blob/master/src/ansiblecmdb/ansible.py#L177, the path shown in the debug message doesn't match the one used for the rest of the method.

This leads to inconsistent behavior: if I pass an inventory without a trailing slash (eg. `-i /home/user/inventories/inventory-prod`), it logs "Parsing host vars (dir): /home/user/inventories/inventory-prod/group_vars", but the `path` is `/home/user/inventories/group_vars`.

I tried a quick fix, changing these lines to something like
```python
path = os.path.join(inventory_path, 'group_vars')
self.log.debug("Parsing group vars (dir): {0}".format(path))
```
fixes the issue, while also not doing the "almost same but wrong sometimes" calculation in debug print and in variable affectation.

issue_title: Concurrency issues
issue_body:
I noticed the code [here](https://github.com/tomasbasham/ratelimit/blob/1f921dedc8286363e5c355ba0188b6ed6a95b740/ratelimit/__init__.py#L40-L46) has been changed since my [last commit](https://github.com/tomasbasham/ratelimit/blob/ea16e71bb635499ff5701f20817391fda246ed94/ratelimit/__init__.py#L32-L38). 

I do realize that my version will probably block 2 or more threads from accessing the API call at the same time, thus compromising my intention of multithreading. However, it seems that the new code might have issues on that subject too.

            with lock:
                elapsed = time.time() - last_called[0]
            left_to_wait = frequency - elapsed
            if left_to_wait > 0:
                time.sleep(left_to_wait)
            ret = func(*args, **kargs)
            last_called[0] = time.time()
            return ret

The above code only locks the operation `elapsed = time.time() - last_called[0]`, but not the operation `last_called[0] = time.time()`. Now, for example, if `func` call is an operation that blocks for 20 seconds (maybe due to slow API communications), and my frequency is 1 second, and I have 10 threads at the same time. What is going to happen?

The first thread will acquire the lock, calculate the elapsed, release the lock, run `func`, which will block for 20 seconds. Note that last_called[0] will not be updated during the 20 seconds! Therefore, during the 20 seconds, all of my other 9 threads will be able to acquire and release the lock, call func. If more than 2 threads call `func` in one second, the effect of the rate limiter will be compromised. 

Therefore, I propose the [change](https://github.com/tomasbasham/ratelimit/pull/8). 

Note that I [moved](https://github.com/ginward/ratelimit/blob/0505cdb0f687b3b9f60923672462a598a7dfc043/ratelimit/__init__.py#L41-L45) the last_called[0] within the lock structure. The advantage is that if func blocks for a bit, the threads won't block. 

I tested the code with my code: 
```
import time
import threading
@rate_limited(2, 1)
def increment():
    '''
    Increment the counter at most once
    every 2 seconds.
    '''
    time.sleep(5)
    print time.time()

thread1 = threading.Thread(target = increment)
thread2 = threading.Thread(target = increment)
thread3 = threading.Thread(target = increment)

thread1.start()
thread2.start()
thread3.start()
```
The output of the `old commit `is: 

`1502028965.591502028965.591502028965.59`

The output of my` new commit` is: 

```
1502029318.34
1502029318.84
1502029319.35
```
Apparently, all three threads called the same function at the same seconds in the old commit, which broke the limit. While the new commit ensures the limit.

issue_title: Trouble sending XML in body when using POST
issue_body:
TL;DR  the POST keyword eventually calls request.request using json= parameter for the body.  Yet for XML the body is not in JSON format. 

Detailed explanation of what I think is root cause along with sample code can be found [here](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/50771356/how-do-i-send-xml-in-the-body-of-a-post-using-robotframework-restinstance)

Robot file to recreate issue:
```
*** Settings ***
Library    REST

*** Variables ***
${SERVER_URL}    https://www.w3schools.com/xml/tempconvert.asmx

*** Test Cases ***
Send XML Body test
    Send SOAP via POST    <?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?><soap:Envelope xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/"><soap:Body><FahrenheitToCelsius xmlns="https://www.w3schools.com/xml/"><Fahrenheit>100</Fahrenheit></FahrenheitToCelsius></soap:Body></soap:Envelope>


*** Keywords ***
Send SOAP via POST
    [Arguments]         ${body}
    SET HEADERS    {"SOAPAction": "https://www.w3schools.com/xml/FahrenheitToCelsius","Content-Type": "text/xml","Cache-Control": "no-cache"}

    ${resp}=    POST    ${SERVER_URL}    data=${body}
    Log    ${resp}  repr=true

```

Python code to prove endpoint works with Sample POST:
```
import requests

url = "https://www.w3schools.com/xml/tempconvert.asmx"

payload = '<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?><soap:Envelope xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/"><soap:Body><FahrenheitToCelsius xmlns="https://www.w3schools.com/xml/"><Fahrenheit>100</Fahrenheit></FahrenheitToCelsius></soap:Body></soap:Envelope>'
headers = {
    'SOAPAction': "\"https://www.w3schools.com/xml/FahrenheitToCelsius\"",
    'Content-Type': "text/xml",
    'Cache-Control': "no-cache",
    }

response = requests.request("POST", url, data=payload, headers=headers)

print(response.text)
```

issue_title: Setting plotting style does not work
issue_body:
Hello,

I am trying to configure `Jupyter Notebook ` in order for it do display matplotlib plots according to the current theme.

I tried executing whats mentioned on the `README` inside a cell from the notebook:
```python
# import jtplot submodule from jupyterthemes
from jupyterthemes import jtplot

# currently installed theme will be used to
# set plot style if no arguments provided
jtplot.style()
```
... but an error appears stating that `jtplot` does not exist --> `ImportError: cannot import name 'jtplot'`

1. Why doesn't `jtplot` exist ?
2. I got 2 environments, one with _py3_ and other with _py2_. `jupyterthemes` does import in _py3_ but does not import in _py2_. However, I can configure the themes using `jt -t monokai` in both of my environments. Why can't I import `jupyterthemes` in my _py2_ environment ?
3. Where should I put he line of code `jtplot.style()` for it to be automatically executed each time I open a jupyter notebook. I know this is described on the README file but I really don't know where can I find the `~/.ipython/profile_default/startup/startup.ipy` ?

issue_title: Drop support for h5py < 2.3
issue_body:
As of right now, this package supports h5py >= 2.1, though some functionality is lost. 2.2 gives a bit more, and 2.3 gives full functionality (big item is in Issue #3 ).

Dropping h5py < 2.3 will mean all the conditionals that have to check for h5py version and make the package behave differently can be dropped.

The currently supported h5py versions were released on the following dates according to its github repository

Version | Release Date
------- | ------------
2.1.3 | 2013-04-22
2.2 | 2013-09-04
2.3 | 2014-04-22
2.4 | 2015-01-06
2.5 | 2015-04-09
2.6 | 2016-02-02

Version 2.3 was releases almost 2.5 years ago, so dropping 2.2 and 2.1 is dropping rather deprecated versions. The one downside to doing this is that 2.2 is still the default in the package managers of some still current GNU/Linux distributions including some popular ones (e.g. Debian Jessie, which is what I use right now). So perhaps it is not time to drop 2.1 and 2.2 support just yet. It will be done eventually, though.

issue_title: Set the correct Content-Type for file - GCloud backend
issue_body:
gcloud backend uploads the file with default Content-Type so it is set to `application/octet-stream` on Google Cloud Storage.
Use this API https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/stable/storage-blobs.html#google.cloud.storage.blob.Blob.content_type to set the correct type (at least for `.css` and `.js` files).

issue_title: Defining an __eq__ method on LoLException caused it to become unhashable
issue_body:
Relevant source: https://github.com/pseudonym117/Riot-Watcher/blob/master/riotwatcher/riotwatcher.py#L179-L188

The biggest problem w/this for me -- although I imagine it could apply to a lot of other users -- is illustrated by the following example:

```
import logging
from riorwatcher.riotwatcher import RiotWatcher, LoLException

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)
rw = RiotWatcher(api_key)

try:
    result = rw.get_summoner(name='foo', region='bar')
except LoLException as e:
    logger.exception('something happen')

# --- Logging error ---
# *** TypeError: unhashable type: 'LoLException'
```

The `exception` method of python logger objects automatically includes the raised exception in output.

However, LoLException is unhashable, which causes a `Logging error` to occur.

I have never run into an issue like this before, but a cursory googling reveals the following solution:
http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1608842/types-that-define-eq-are-unhashable-in-python-3-x

Sure enough, removing the `__eq__` and `__ne__` methods from LoLException makes the exception loggable via `logger.exception`. To paraphrase the SO answer, if you wish to keep `__eq__` the solution seems like simply defining `__hash__`.

Official docs on `__hash__`: https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__hash__

_As an aside, this is also problematic for task queues like `celery` that must work w/raised exceptions during the processing of jobs, e.g. to retry a task that failed due to a rate limit exceeded error, etc._

issue_title: Timeouts on Parallel Requests
issue_body:
Hi, 
We're using django 1.10 with Postgresql 9.2 and python 3.5
We have decided to use your package since we need field auto-incremental that is not PK (for some model), and it should be unique per other model.
The sequences table is within our models DB.

It seemed like the solution was good, until we started getting parallel requests. In that case - looks like the DB got locked for too long and we started getting timeouts (We are configured to 10 seconds timeout by our api gateway (Apigee)

Is that behavior expected or the package should handle those cases?
Thanks.

issue_title: logging 2 bots into 2 different files is not working
issue_body:
I have two bots, which are working via webhooks with router on flask, so my structure is:

- app.py - router
- bot1.py
- bot2.py

Router imports bot1 and bot2 and then run the needed bot.

In both bots i configured logging like this:
```
handler = RotatingFileHandler('BOT_NAME.log', maxBytes=18000, backupCount=7) # instead of "BOT_NAME" i write the name of the bot
logging.getLogger("TeleBot").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
logging.getLogger("TeleBot").addHandler(handler)
```

The problem is, that all debug messages from bots goes to both log files, but i need every bot to write debug messages to its own log file. I tried to configure logging with something like this:
```

logging.getLogger("TeleBot.BOT_NAME").setLevel(logging.DEBUG)
logging.getLogger("TeleBot.BOT_NAME").addHandler(handler)
```

But with this code bots don't write debug messages at all.

Is this a bug? Is there a way to configure separated logs for bots?

issue_title: Infinite scrolling instead of pagination (as an option)
issue_body:
It would be nice to have Infinite Scrolling built-in into Zinnia as an alternative to traditional pagination of entry lists.

* a settings option `ZINNIA_INFINITE_SCROLLING = (True|False)` could be introduced 
* the value of `ZINNIA_PAGINATION` should be re-used for the number of items to load initially and when reaching the bottom of the page

## Possible Solution Pieces

* Implementing a simple `ListView` returning a sliced result, e.g.
```
    start = page * settings.ZINNIA_PAGINATION
    entries = ...
    entries[start:start + settings.ZINNIA_PAGINATION]
```
* Using the unmaintained [infinite-scroll jQuery plugin](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/26600216/how-to-implement-infinite-scroll-endless-pagination-using-the-zinnia-blog-app-f).
* Using the [jScroll – jQuery Plugin for Infinite Scrolling](http://plugins.jquery.com/jscroll/).
* Using [django-endless-pagination](https://github.com/frankban/django-endless-pagination) and its [pagination on scroll](http://django-endless-pagination.readthedocs.org/en/latest/twitter_pagination.html#pagination-on-scroll).

This is a suggestion for an improvement. I'd like the xls format output to set the cell background color when using xlwt.

I forked the project and made a solution that works for my needs. I'm not going to post a PR since I don't think it's a good enough solution for widespread use and I only changed the code related to xlwt so it will break with the other xls libraries.

Here's my branch with my solution :
[https://github.com/samaust/django-tables2-reports/tree/xlwt_background-color](https://github.com/samaust/django-tables2-reports/tree/xlwt_background-color)

If I have a column called "columnname", I also add another column called "columnname_color". My branch searches for those color columns and adds the colors to the custom Excel palette.

issue_title: Cannot be imported on Python 3.6, because of keyword-only argument inspection
issue_body:
jsonpatch==1.14 on Python 3.6 cannot be imported.

It fails with the following stacktrace:

```python
      .tox/py/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jsonpatch.py:114: in <module>
          json.load = get_loadjson()
      .tox/py/lib/python3.6/site-packages/jsonpatch.py:108: in get_loadjson
          argspec = inspect.getargspec(json.load)
      lib/python3.6/inspect.py:1039: in getargspec
          raise ValueError("Function has keyword-only arguments or annotations"
      E   ValueError: Function has keyword-only arguments or annotations, use getfullargspec() API which can support them
```

The error message is a bit misleading. In Python >=3.5, `inspect.getfullargspec()` is also deprecated, in favor of `inspect.signature()`.

https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/inspect.html#inspect.getfullargspec

This happened because Python 3.6 changed all optional arguments to be keyword-only.

https://docs.python.org/3.6/library/json.html#json.load

issue_title: Error with Django 1.9
issue_body:
When using the django-unixtimestampfield with Django version 1.9 the following exception was thrown:

ValidationError: [u"Unable to convert value: '1424849146' to python data type"] @



    File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/db/models/sql/compiler.py", line 796, in apply_converters

    File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/unixtimestampfield/submiddleware.py", line 15, in field_value_middleware
      return field.to_timestamp(value) if field.use_numeric else field.to_datetime(value)

issue_title: MXNet on Spark Roadmap
issue_body:
https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/pull/2256 makes MXNet on Spark possible. It works on a stable Spark cluster, but when it is brought to a complex environment, e.g., executors may fail and retry, multiple tasks may configured to run in one executor, etc.

Related to issue https://github.com/dmlc/mxnet/issues/1637 @tqchen 

Here's a roadmap for all those issues which may prevent from using MXNet on Spark in production environment.
- [x] KVStore workers (and scheduler/servers) failover. I found that, when a worker fails and restarts, it is not able to connect the scheduler again. Is it expected for ps-lite? @mli 
- [x] Timeout for ps scheduler and servers. PS scheduler and servers are started in a spawned process. But when something goes wrong, e.g., workers, servers or the scheduler crash, they will have no chance to stop themselves. I think the simplest way to solve this problem is to set a timeout T, if the scheduler/server does not receive any message in T seconds, stop itself.
- [ ] Multiple ps-lite threads in one process. Currently ps-lite is singleton, but Spark clusters can be configured to run multiple tasks in one executor (related to one process).
- [ ] Make device resources transparent to application. Instead of specifying which GPU to use, users only need to know how many GPUs they want. This is important for clusters which use Yarn to do resource management.
- [x] Upload and distribute MXNet core library to all the worker nodes automatically.

issue_title: Difficult to get/save symbols in new API mxnet.module
issue_body:
I've been using MXNET for a while and get used to the interface mxnet.model.FeedForward, which has a method save_checkpoint(). This method returns a JSON file of symbol definition and .params for saving args and aux params. But with the new API mxnet.module I can't figure out how to get/save the symbol easily in this module, and it makes more complex to use or finetune on models based on this interface, since loading a model requires a JSON file for symbol definition.

In `mxnet.module.BaseModule` there's a property `symbol` which allows us to get the symbol in module. But in other inherit classes like `mxnet.module.Module` and `mxnet.module.SequentialModule`, we can comprise/stack modules to build more complex networks. This makes harder to access the whole symbol in these modules, even though we can get symbols of each submodules in recursive way and concat them, but this is still not straightforward for me.

issue_title: Periodic Loss Value when training with "step" learning rate policy
issue_body:
When training deep CNN, a common way is to use **SGD with momentum with a "step" learning rate policy**(e.g. learning rate set to be 0.1,0.01,0.001.. at different stages of training).But I encounter an unexpected phenomenon  when training with this strategy under MXNet.


**That is the periodic training loss value** 
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26757001/31327825-356401b6-ad04-11e7-9aeb-3f690bc50df2.png)
**The above is the training loss at a fixed learning rate 0.01, where the loss is decreasing normally**
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/26757001/31327872-8093c3c4-ad04-11e7-8fbd-327b3916b278.png)
**However, at the second stage of training(with lr 0.001) , the loss goes up and down periodically, and the period is exactly an epoch**


**So I thought it might be the problem of data shuffling**, but it cannot explain why it doesn't happen in the first stage.  Actually I used ImageRecordIter as the DataIter and reset it after every epoch, is there anything I missed or set mistakenly 
```
train_iter = mx.io.ImageRecordIter(
	path_imgrec=recPath,
	data_shape=dataShape,
	batch_size=batchSize,
	last_batch_handle='discard',
	shuffle=True,
	rand_crop=True,
	rand_mirror=True)
```

The codes for training and loss evaluation
```
while True:
	train_iter.reset()
	for i,databatch in enumerate(train_iter):
                globalIter += 1
		mod.forward(databatch,is_train=True)
		mod.update_metric(metric,databatch.label)
		if globalIter % 100 == 0:
                    loss = metric.get()[1]
                    metric.reset()
                mod.backward()
                mod.update()
```
Actually the loss can converge, but it takes too long.
I've suffered from this problem for a long period of time, on different network and different datasets.
I didn't have this problem when using Caffe.  Is this due to the implementation difference?



Anyone can give some suggestions?

issue_title: plone.app.contenttypes 1.1.x depends plone.app.event [plone43]
issue_body:
It is said in [README.rst](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.contenttypes/blob/master/docs/README.rst) the plone.app.contenttypes 1.1.x is compatible with Plone 4.3.x and plone.app.contenttypes 1.2.x only works with Plone 5.

plone.app.contenttypes 1.1.x has [plone.app.event [dexterity] as dependency](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.contenttypes/blob/1.1.x/setup.py#L42).

However, plone.app.event only [install plone.app.widgets if the plone.app.event [plone43]](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.event/blob/master/setup.py#L103-L104) is installed. And plone.app.event need [plone.app.widgets in Plone 4.3.x](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.event/blob/master/plone/app/event/configure.zcml#L27). [plone.app.event/bbb/configure.zcml](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.event/blob/master/plone/app/event/bbb/configure.zcml#L6)

As plone.app.contenttypes 1.1.x works in Plone 4.3.x, it must depend of plone.app.event [dexterity plone43] and not plone.app.event [dexterity]

issue_title: --no-latest-check and good defaults
issue_body:
So in our initial toe-dipping investigations we had discovered when running sops on hardware inside our corporate network, it would take > 30 seconds to execute, but from elsewhere it would take < 1 second. It turns out the root cause is due to us not declaring the `--no-latest-check` argument when running it, as the method it calls is not aware of common proxy environment variables (`https_proxy` and the likes) and the wait is due to proxy timeout, in which case it gives up and just completes the task anyhow.

We could just send a pull request adding proxy support to the check, however I think now is a good time to have a discussion about wether or not the idea of a security product like sops 'phoning home' on every execution by default is a good idea and that it outweighs the risks of out-of-date and potentially vulnerable software being exploited. A very large number of our machines will be automatically executing this code frequently as well as engineers and not necessarily be in an immediate position to upgrade the moment a new release is made available. At the same time we could look at other options - disabling the check everywhere or forking code being the first to mind.

Is this the right default to have?

issue_title: An idea: more efficient encoding of homogeneous sequences
`notify` instead of `notify_change`?

Should we `notify` or `notify_change`?

It seems like `notify_change` might be confusing as the types of notifications being used start to diversify. For instance, I can imagine passing a simple `Bunch(name='something', type='ping')` that doesn't signify a "change".

Of course, this wouldn't be backward compatible, but a simple regex pattern would suffice to account for this change downstream. If this change is deemed reasonable, one should not that `traitlets` still uses `_notify_change` internally, and that people may not have migrated to `notify_change` yet, so now would be the best time to make this change. issue_title: Autocompletion of compiled/large modules is taking too long
issue_body:
We're currently developing an LSP extension for Spyder https://github.com/spyder-ide/spyder/pull/4751 and we're using your server to test all the LSP features, and eventually we'll be using it by default. 

At the moment we haven't got much trouble migrating our own implemented functionalities, however we have noticed that some ``textDocument/completion`` requests take too long to be replied by the server, especially if they are related to modules such as numpy. On such cases we've experienced about 3-5 delay seconds.

Right now on Spyder we've been using both Jedi and Rope to handle our autocompletion calls, as Rope is faster than Jedi in resolving autocompletions for large/compiled modules, we achieve autocompletion in less than a second, which is one of the features more beloved by the Spyder user community.

We would like to contribute to improve the autocompletion performance of the server, we propose to use both Jedi and Rope to handle LSP completion requests, the idea is to define a race condition between both packages, by returning the results of the package that completes the autocompletion task first.

We would like to hear your opinion on this solution and discuss other alternatives, as Spyder LSP migration is imminent.

issue_title: Failure when list contains a field named 'list'
issue_body:
I have a field with this definition:
```xml
<Field xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/sharepoint/soap/" xmlns:soap="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/soap/envelope/" xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xmlns:xsd="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema" Type="Text" DisplayName="list" Required="TRUE" EnforceUniqueValues="TRUE" Indexed="TRUE" MaxLength="255" ID="{5959fc0c-1c7e-43c7-b445-e4cfa79dd46f}" SourceID="{3565187c-c2a3-45d8-84dc-6b9cc5c7bd5c}" StaticName="list" Name="list" ColName="nvarchar6" RowOrdinal="0"/>
```

This breaks in various ways because it ends up replacing the `SharePointListRow.list` attribute which is otherwise a reference back to the list.

As a start, an assert could be added to not accept fields named `fields`, `list` or `opener`:

https://github.com/ox-it/python-sharepoint/blob/f1a1e19189d78115fcfc25850d27319e34d7e699/sharepoint/lists/__init__.py#L197

A better solution would probably be to rename the `list` attribute to something else.

issue_title: django 1.9.x compatibility

with django 1.9.2 I run into an ImportError:

> File "/[path-to-site-packages]/django_libs/format_utils.py", line 19, in <module>
>     from django.utils.importlib import import_module
> ImportError: No module named importlib


In the given django version there is no importlib module anymore.
A small change like the following did the trick (for me):

```
try:
    from importlib import import_module

    from django.utils.importlib import import_module

hope this helps

issue_title: Support cache regeneration when switching b/w typelibs older than the comtypes cache
issue_body:
_(This is rather an odd requirement so I don't mind if this is not supported.)_

I have to work with comm dlls which don't use the version numbers at all, To be more specific, the version numbers are _always_ hardcoded as 1.0 and newer version's of the dlls (for reasons not under my control) don't update their version numbers. So, If I switch from a recent version of the dll/tlb to an older version, the comtypes cache is not regenerated. So I suggest to compare the timestamp of the current tlb vs the timestamp of tlb used for code generation while determining if the cache has to be re-generated instead of the current practice of comparing the time stamps of the current tlb vs the cache.

issue_title: When setting minio using https and port 80 python sdk will connect to port 443

By using python sdk to connect minio server with specified http_client, I got connection error.
After investigation, I found minio/helpers line 224 striped port from endpoint_url：
    # Strip 80/443 ports since curl & browsers do not
    # send them in Host header.
    if parsed_url.port == 80 or parsed_url.port == 443:
        host = parsed_url.hostname
Since using http://your.url.com:80 will get same behavior by browser, suggests remove this strip from code to let user can using this two port in abnormal way.

Thanks

issue_title: BatchingBolt which is volume-based, not time-based.
issue_body:
The current BatchingBolt implementation is based on the number of ticks. A variant could accumulate tuples (or even values contained in those tuples) until a certain threshold is hit. This would be useful when e.g. batching to an upstream/downstream service that is rate-limited, as to send a full request.

This would likely need some time-delay featured in which the threshold is ignored after a certain wait, as to avoid holding onto values indefinitely if the threshold is never hit.

issue_title: PycurlClient doesn't work with Python 3
issue_body:
When using the Stripe Python API in combination with PycurlClient and Python 3, the requests made to the API cannot complete.

In the `http_client.py` file, the first line of the `_handle_request_error` function the value of e is `error(23, 'Failed writing header')`. The error results in another error because the request handling code doesn't work with this specific error value (the error object does not support indexing).

The cause of this problem seems the be the use if StringIO instead of BytesIO:
https://stackoverflow.com/a/23610279/138103

To test run any method that connects to the API in Python 3 after running the following:
```python
stripe.default_http_client = stripe.http_client.PycurlClient()
```

Python Version: 3.5.3
Library Version: 1.55.2

issue_title: Cap'n Proto schema should be compiled into shared library
issue_body: The Python shared libraries may be loaded by the same executable. If multiple shared libraries compile in the same capnp schema then there will be an error. We should instead dynamically link against a schema-only shared library.

issue_title: Building python bindings doesn't fail when the shared objects aren't linked properly
issue_body:
When building python bindings, even though the build may be successful, if it can't find the shared libraries for `python2.7`, `kj` and `capnp` you will get an `ImportError` when trying to use the bindings from python.

Please add the following entry to [bindings/py/setup.py](https://github.com/numenta/nupic.core/blob/master/bindings/py/setup.py#L358) `commonLinkFlags`:

```
"-Wl,--no-undefined",
```

Additionally, this turned up the error that `kj` was not being found, I fixed it by adding another linker and compiler flag (this was because I did not attempt to install capnproto in a system folder, which I think is what happens when you build `nupic.core`):
```
"-L/tmp/capnproto-c++-0.5.3/build/lib"
```

issue_title: RFC: translations split and use of https://www.transifex.com/
issue_body:
##### Current status (in next/pu)

Following libraries Babel and Flask-Babel are used:
- http://babel.pocoo.org/docs/cmdline/
- https://pythonhosted.org/Flask-Babel/

##### Problems

1. `invenio.pot` is huge and there is missing support overlay translations (see #828)
1. compiling translations after installation with `pybabel compile -fd invenio/base/translations/` command (one needs to get to source directory)
1. Flask-Babel does not allow easy domain switch (see fork Flask-BabelEx: https://pythonhosted.org/Flask-BabelEx/)
1. it can be difficult for non-tech translators to contribute to Invenio

##### Proposal

- [x] use domains and/or split translations loading per module (see #1984)
- [x] compile translations before creating zip/tar.gz
- [x] evaluate use of Flask-BabelEx or take part in improving Flask-Babel
- [x] create project on https://www.transifex.com/ (Indico is there)

issue_title: morningstar reader does not increment retry count
issue_body:
I have a program that is failing on two symbols. AAXN and AABA. It creates a stack overflow error eventually.

As far as I can tell it's because retry count never gets incremented. And then it recursively calls itself to try again.

issue_title: Detecting if is_authenticated is a method causes deprecation warnings
issue_body:
Note the implementation you made for the client [here](https://github.com/getsentry/raven-python/blob/d8d3c834b7d3792bdef93a55e876ace1630579c6/raven/contrib/django/client.py#L150)

This is a problem because it will cause a deprecation warning every time the module runs a logger. 

I have a special case where I have to manage a large website. I use raven to send logging events when it detects a deprecation warning, this will cause it to send a deprecation warning event because of how this was implemented.
http://i.imgur.com/drrKVYt.png

Since technically in Django 1.11, `is_authenticated()` is still valid until it is fully phased out in 2.0.

Wouldn't a check for this be more effected like:
```python
            if hasattr(user, 'is_authenticated'):
                authenticated = user.is_authenticated
            elif callable(user.is_authenticated):
                # Django < 1.11
                authenticated = user.is_authenticated()
            elif not authenticated:
                return None
```

issue_title: Windows / Appveyor - same version of cached file - invalidated and built  again
issue_body:
I am using `pip-accel` with appveyor in my project and `pip-accel` invalidates even the same version of a package that is already cached:
```
pip_accel.bdist    INFO Invalidating old numpy (1.10.1) binary (source is newer) ..
pip_accel.bdist    INFO Building numpy (1.10.1) binary distribution ..
```
Url for appveyor build: https://ci.appveyor.com/project/matysek/pyinstaller-ewwmo/build/181/job/6aj9u6f8lsfbpej9

In the build appveyor is instructed to cache these paths:
```
C:\Users\appveyor\AppData\Local\pip
C:\Users\appveyor\AppData\Roaming\.pip-accel
```

* I've seen already similar issue when porting `pip-accel` to windows.
* My workaround was to ignore the `mtime`.
* It looks like `mtime` is not reliable when appveyor caches some files to any external storage between builds.

issue_title: Add Error Reporting Library
issue_body:
This issue is to separately track some of the scope creep from #1881. 

We need:
- A client to talk to the Error Reporting API. Currently this API does not seem public yet, so until then, we can create Error Reports by talking to the Logging API in a [specific structured format.](https://cloud.google.com/error-reporting/docs/formatting-error-messages) Once Error Reporting API exists we migrate the implementation from Logging API to Error Reporting API ( so it has its own quota, auth, etc).
- A logging handler that, for messages of severity error, gets the stacktrace/function context and reports to Error Reporting API
- A hook that lets you setup a global Python exception handler to catch exceptions and properly report the context.
- A similar hook for Django/Flask apps that will report the exception context and also include HTTP context.

issue_title: Complete support for variants
issue_body:
The native engine currently has a few stubbings that prevent Variants from working (marked with TODOs mentioning this ticket):

1. Variant merging is implemented by `core::Variants`, but not used.
2. Requesting the `Variants` product is implemented by `nodes::Select`, but not merging them (in particular: this will either require lifting the user-defined variants into rust for merging, or pushing down rust's variants to python for merging)
3. `nodes::Dependencies` does not parse Variants from Address objects (the new `path/to:target@key=value` syntax).

issue_title: Improve the error message when a directory is missing while looking up a transitive dependency
issue_body:
imagine a layout like

a/aa/BUILD:
```
target(
  dependencies=["b/aa"]
)
```
b/bb/BUILD:
```
target()
```

Currently, running `./pants list a::` will result in an error like
```
ResolveError: "aa" was not found in namespace "b/aa". Did you mean one of:


```

It would be better if it instead said something like

```
ResolveError: "aa" was not found in namespace "b/aa". Namespace "b/aa" declares no targets.
```
Additionally, there's a bunch of trace info that shows up, I think because it's not a handled resolve error.

issue_title: Update clean-all to have the ability to cleanup all artifacts
issue_body:
./pants clean-all only kills nailgun and deletes .pants.d
users often have problems that are solved by deleting .buildcache or sometimes even ~/.pants.d or ~/.cache or ~/.ivy2
Having to manually delete this list of things (which has changed a couple times a year since I started here, and many twitter vets are unaware of) is crappy and undiscoverable.
./pants clean-all should have options to kill more and more things, so that users can easily escalate as needed.
This would also solve the problem of "users run clean-all, build, fail, rm -rf .buildcache, build, fail" where .pants.d and .buildcache reinfect each other if you don't do them at the same time.

issue_title: Java analysis is empty after a noop
issue_body:
When jvm_compile invokes zinc for a noop on a Java target, the second build can deterministically result in empty analysis for the Java target.
An easy way to reproduce is by adding an unused dependency to a java_library, and then compiling it twice in a row with:
./pants compile.zinc --unused-deps=fatal $target
The first time you will see an accurate error for the unused dep. The second time (without changes!) you will see all deps of the library reported as unused (because the analysis is now empty).

Just a quick look shows there is a difference in the analysis files after a noop. Tested with a compile that generated 31 analysis files. After a noop there were 3 analysis files that shrunk in size  out of 31 analysis files generated (e.g. 105K->23K in size)

issue_title: CodeUri should accept path outside of the current region
issue_body:
Hello,

Since S3 is a global resource, it seems odd that there is a restriction that a CodeUri path must be in the same region as a Function. It seems logical that I can make `s3://some_bucket/artifact.zip` a publically accessible resource and then anyone should be able to use it from any region.

There are also inconsistencies within the AWS ecosystem on this practice. For example, a nested stack (`AWS::CloudFormation::Stack`) cloudformation template can be in any region (publically accessible) and then you use a `https://s3.amazonaws.com/...` url

issue_title: print 'time spent' at the end of each makefile command
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

In #3776 we're trying to speed up `make dist`, but it's a little hard to measure the time the command takes.

### Expected behavior

In general, it would be nice to print out time spent in commands so we can easily check whether certain changes speed them up.

### User-facing consequences

None for users, but developers would have a better sense of how their changes affect tooling performance


### Context

0.10.0

issue_title: Language switching needs a tidy-up
issue_body:
[] When switching between languages, you can lose entered data without warning - django CMS warns you "Are you sure you want to change tabs without saving the page first?"
[] the language switch tabs are differently-styled from django CMS's
[] the `name` and slug fields are not translated but appear in all language tabs, whereas the `function` field in-between them is translated - but there's nothing to indicate these different behaviours

Maybe names *should* be translatable - after all a name in Hebrew or Arabic is not the same as in English or German.

issue_title: Bootstrap Confidant's secrets
issue_body:
Confidant's web interface requires secrets, so we should ensure those don't need to live in the clear. Easiest option here is:

1. Add a script that can use Confidant's at-rest KMS key that'll read from a json (or yaml) file (or stdin) encrypt/base64 the contents and output a file (or write to stdout)
2. Modify settings.py to read the encrypted contents, decrypt it, then load it as a dict.
3. Read the settings from the dict.

1hour suds request timeout?
issue_body:
Hey there,

We've got a small subset of Adwords requests which seem to hang sometimes, and we have a suspicion it is coming from the 1 hour timeout set on the request:

https://github.com/googleads/googleads-python-lib/blob/master/googleads/adwords.py#L380-L390

Is there any way you can add this as an option to make it configurable? We could manually patch it but we'd prefer to use a documented API here.

issue_title: Edit mode not preserving things properly
issue_body:
Entering and then immediately leaving edit mode on a worksheet doesn't seem to preserve formatting.

Example:
```
$ test_cmd=$(cat <<EOF
> echo hello
> echo hello2
> EOF
> )

$ cl run -n test_break --- "$test_cmd"
0xad88a05b2d1141338425ef6b6b2c6060

$ cl cat ^1
name    perm  size
------------------
stderr  0644     0
stdout  0644    13

$ cl cat ^1/stdout
hello
hello2
```
Notice that the `test_cmd` will contain a newline since there was no backslash at the end of the `echo hello` line.

Even with this extra newline, the bundle looks ok in the worksheet.

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2111202/24822710/2273f294-1bac-11e7-9c07-c5e04829b535.png)

However, if you go into edit mode (even if you don't change anything) and then save out of it, the formatting of the bundle breaks:

![image](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/2111202/24822737/6ba9b66a-1bac-11e7-99bb-08371f6aee18.png)

Presumably because it now "sees" the extraneous newline.

issue_title: SecurityRule 'any' attributes inconsistent behavior
issue_body: `policies.SecurityRule` has certain attributes that default to the string value 'any'. However when a SecurityRule is created from a live device and that rule has attributes actually set to 'any' the value is represented as a list `['any']`. This is inconsistent behavior and leads to extra checks by the developer to deal with these attributes when their value is actually set to 'any'.

[...]/profitbricks/client.py in create_lan(self, datacenter_id, lan)

issue_title: Following suggestions for image.save results in really slow saves
issue_body:
I'm trying to write out an image which has a modest number of unionfs layers (5) and which is pretty small when I do docker image ls (~200MiB). The code I use to save the image looks like this:

```py
    with open(outputImageFileName, 'wb', buffering=0) as outputImage:
        with deployImage.save() as imageBytes:
            for chunk in tqdm(imageBytes, leave=True, miniters=1):
                outputImage.write(chunk)
```

Saving the image this way takes well upwards of 28 minutes on a brand new MacBook pro with plenty RAM and big SSD whereas from the command line it is nearly instant. 

For what it's worth, when I tried to do a little detective work, I found that the current version of urllib3 has arguments to the call to `stream` which, amongst other things, makes [it possible to control the size of chunks being transferred](http://urllib3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/#urllib3.response.HTTPResponse.stream); however, this interface does not appear to be supported on the  object that are returned by `Image.save()`

issue_title: Feature request: multiple levels entrypoints
issue_body:
I would be nice to have "multiple levels" entrypoints. For example, looking at disqus wrapper' [resource_mapping](https://github.com/marctc/tapioca-disqus/tree/master/tapioca_disqus/resource_mapping), we see that we have many files and they actually correspond to prefixes in the urls. 

The way tapioca is today forces us to repeat the prefix in the resource_mapping dict and in the resource url. 

Instead of accessing the entrypoints as `api.blacklists_add` and `api.blacklists_remove`, we would access it as `api.blacklists.add` and `api.blacklists.remove`.

[Tortilla](https://github.com/redodo/tortilla) does this, but that repo does not seems to support oauth2 and does not seem to be really active / maintained.

If you think it's a good idea, I may be able to take a look at the Tortilla to see if it's easy to adapt tapioca's code.

Thanks

Connecting to crossbar router started after component regularly fails
issue_body:
**Scenario**
A running application with pretty minimal code to just have a `Component` instance connecting the router.

The router is not running. It is started some seconds after the appliction.

**Expected behavior**
Initially the app obviously fails to connect to the router, when retrying it can eventually connect once the router is up.

**Observed behavior**
In a lot of cases no `join` event is fired at all. the component just stops to reconnect.

Find below the code and the log output.

```python
import os

from autobahn.asyncio.component import Component, run


def create_component():
    authentication = None

    component = Component(
        transports=[
            {
                "type": "websocket",
                "url": os.environ.get("AUTOBAHN_ROUTER", "ws://localhost:8080/ws"),
            }
        ],
        authentication=authentication,
        realm="realm1",
    )

    return component


class Application:
    def __init__(self, component):
        self.component = component

        component.on("join", self.on_join)
        component.on("leave", self.on_leave)

    async def on_join(self, session, details):
        print("JUST JOINED")

    async def on_leave(self, session, reason):
        print("JUST LEFT")


def main():
    component = create_component()

    app = Application(component)

    run([component], log_level="debug")


if __name__ == "__main__":

issue_title: guard against using unspecified GPUs
issue_body:
When transferring data onto GPU, it would be helpful if the library would guard against using unspecified GPUs based on the specified GPU in the config file, i.e.

```python
with torch.cuda.device(cuda_device):
   # all cuda data transfers
```
otherwise, some data can wind up being transferred onto an unspecified device (default GPU), which could be used by another process at the same time.

I experienced some related issues, e.g. GPU0 was being used, I specified GPU5, some of the model was transferred onto GPU0, and there was an out of memory error.

Related, it would also be nice if the library could check whether the specified GPU is available before loading data rather than after.

issue_title: Validation for URI is inconsistent with tablechema-js if field value is IP
issue_body:
Not sure which one of this is correct - is IP URI or not - but there is a difference between how `Schema` modules work in `tableschema-py` and `tablechema-js`.  For example if you try and cast `'19.16.162.96'` with `type=string` and `format=uri` in JS it works fine:

```
const {Schema} = require('tableschema')
let schema = await Schema.load({'fields': [{'name': 'ip', 'type': 'string', 'format': 'uri'}]})
schema.castRow(['19.16.162.96'])
```

But if you try and do same in python it will fail with cast error: `can't cast value "19.16.162.96" for type "string" with format "uri"`

```
from tableschema import Schema
schema = Schema({'fields': [{'name': 'ip', 'type': 'string', 'format': 'uri'}]})
schema.cast_row(['19.16.162.96'])
```

This gives us some hard times if we use `tableschema-js` library to infer schema from row data and process the data later with python library that uses `tablschema-py` to cast values (like [datapackage-pipelines](https://github.com/frictionlessdata/datapackage-pipelines))

**Note:**  in the earlier version of `tableschema-js` they were aligned. I believe they've split after this commit https://github.com/frictionlessdata/tableschema-js/commit/fce178a113338ceb40966aae80fffe9a5633d158

issue_title: Proposal: Use in-house mirroring for slow internet connections
issue_body:
Docker 1.3 made it fairly simple to use an in-house registry to pull public docker images: https://github.com/docker/docker/blob/master/docs/sources/articles/registry_mirror.md

Providing a `deisctl` command to use an in-house registry when your outbound internet connection is slow, would allow pre-seeding, faster new node boot times and decrease the overall deis download size as layer downloads like ubuntu-debootstrap are shared between nodes.

//cc @aledbf , @jperville

issue_title: Document SDK client classesas unsafe for multiprocessing and not guaranteed to be threadsafe
issue_body:
This came up in the context of a Search user using a process pool to index data in parallel.

Each SDK client object contains a requests Session.
Sessions hold urllib3 connection pools.
Connection pools are documented as threadsafe, but [are known to be unsafe prefork](https://github.com/urllib3/urllib3/issues/850), so creating clients before a fork and using them afterwards is unsafe.

There may be other methods of SDK clients which are not threadsafe and I don't want to tie us in knots worrying about this.

SDK clients should be considered unsafe to create prefork and thread-unsafe.
We should recommend creating your client objects after forking and once-per-process.

Technically, someone could have a use-case for driving multiple client instances off of a single authorizer and want us to improve this to function out of the box, but today it _won't_ and I don't want people getting the wrong idea. If someone has a strong enough driving need, we can consider making them threadsafe at that time.

issue_title: Idea to decouple `PeerPool` and management of peer pool logic.
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Right now we have the base logic for managing peer connections **and** the business logic for managing peer connections coupled.

Base logic is defined as things like the base p2p connection/handshake, `is_full` checks, starting and stopping peers.

Business logic is defined here as things like "preferred peers", dao fork checks, stats reporting, selection of *best* peers (not implemented).

### How can it be fixed

I'm curious if these should be decoupled.  The base logic pretty much never changes, but the business logic is more dynamic.  We could potentially have a separate concept which I'll call *peer pool manager*.  The manager is responsible for implementing these *business logic* components.  This saves us from having to change our peer pool class so often, allowing us to just swap out the manager.

This will require some discovery work to figure out if it's a good idea, but dropping it here just in case.

issue_title: Validate text and hexstr parameters are str type
issue_body:
* Version: 1

### What was wrong?

See [this comment](https://github.com/ethereum/eth-utils/pull/58#discussion_r167601284) about `bytes` being passed to `to_text` as `text=`. In that case `to_text()` returned a `bytes` value, which seems wrong.

### How can it be fixed?

Raise an exception if the `text` or `hexstr` parameters are not `str`, in any of the conversion methods.

issue_title: Activate enable the wrong version of python
issue_body:
This is related to issue #166 which has been opened with the following:

> Having a symlink to conda in every environment would allow to only have that environment's bin directory added to PATH, and would avoid potential confusion.

And closed with

> Each environment has a symlink to conda, activate, and deactivate, and activate removes the root environment from the PATH.

So on a machine which has its own (old) python in /usr/bin running 

`source activate blah`

removes the root environment from PATH (as described above in the close issue #166 message) and that causes the wrong version of python being active (and a bunch of other stuff). I would suggest adding everything else from the root environment to the environment, but of course not removing the root environment may be a sensible choice too

issue_title: [FR] Conda should have more aggressive HTTP caching
issue_body:
It would be nice to have more aggressive HTTP caching in conda (and repo.continuum.io and conda.anaconda.org), especially for the `fetch_index` call that occurs at the beginning of many user interactions. With a couple channels added, this often takes ~1000ms for me.
1. Currently, my understanding is that some caching is used only when the user supplies a special flag (`--use-index-cache`), which I assume is rare. But even this cache is pretty ineffective.
2. On anaconda.org, the `repodata.json.bz2` file is not served with any ETag / Expires  / Cache-Control headers.
3. On `https://repo.continuum.io/`, the `repodata.json.bz2` file is served with ETag, but not Expires or Cache-Control headers. Without those headers, you can't avoid a trip to the server, even if the response is cached.
4. Instead of manually operating a cache, as is done in `fetch_repodata()`, all of this could be done transparently with a library like [CacheControl](http://cachecontrol.readthedocs.org/en/latest/index.html). IIRC, this is what pip does as well. But obviously it requires the right HTTP headers.

issue_title: Sparse local package repo
issue_body:
As I mentioned in #5983 and as seems to be alluded to in #5889, the ability to create a sparse local package repo that could be passed to `conda install` as an option, especially in conjunction with `--offline`, would be an excellent feature I'd like to see added.

A clarifying use case: right now we have a base container image that our applications build on top of. The different applications have different requirements, but we'd like to put a significant subset of packages into the base image and cut down on build time length and variability (as well as reduce our load on anaconda.org infrastructure ;) ).

We do this right now with pip-installed packages right now through having a "wheelhouse", a flat directory of wheels, combined with usage of the [`--find-links` pip flag](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/pip_wheel/#find-links). 

Being able to do the equivalent for our conda-installed would be amazing. Especially if we can specify the metadata file and avoid the minute or so spent downloading and resolving dependencies, for even the simpler conda package installs.

Let me know if there's any clarification I can provide, and thanks again!
