issue_title: Conda --json flag output no longer pure json
issue_body:
I noticed today that an automated script that I often use to programmatically assemble a metapackage broke upon updating conda because the output of `conda info --json anaconda` now includes "Using Anaconda Cloud api site https://api.anaconda.org" at the beginning. This makes it such that you can no longer pipe the raw output into a json loader without detecting this line and removing it. 

This is fine and easy to patch on the consumer side, but I wonder if the new behavior was intentional and whether it shouldn't be more like it was before this change so that the consumer can always expect to get raw json without having to detect and deal with other external metadata.

issue_title: [Bug] Temporary files should be created at temporary dir
issue_body:
Reasons:
* maybe not possible to create them at the current file path (as seen below)
* if access is granted and something goes wrong you have temporary files that likely stay in a non temporary folder

```log
ERROR::pyqode.backend::something went bad with worker <function lint at 0x034C10C0>(data={'path': 'C:\\TEST.COB', 'code': [removed from log], 'encoding': 'cp1252'})
ERROR::pyqode.backend::Traceback (most recent call last):
ERROR::pyqode.backend::  File "c:\dev\pyqode.core\pyqode\core\backend\server.py", line 135, in _handle
ERROR::pyqode.backend::  File "C:\Dev\OpenCobolIDE\open_cobol_ide\linter.py", line 33, in lint
ERROR::pyqode.backend::PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: 'C:\\.oci1433411286.cbl'
```

This is what cobc/libcob does (pseudocode):

```c
if !(tmpdir = getenv ("TMPDIR")) {
#ifdef	_WIN32
   if (tmpdir = !getenv ("TEMP"))
      if !(tmpdir = getenv ("TMP"))
         if !(tmpdir = getenv ("USERPROFILE"))
            tmpdir = .;
#else
   if (tmpdir = !getenv ("TMP"))
      if !(tmpdir = getenv ("TEMP"))
         tmpdir = /tmp;
#endif

   setenv("TMPDIR", tmpdir);
}
```

For supporting relative paths to copybooks it should be enough to pass an additional -I $fullpath_of_current_file to the compiler

## System information


- OpenCobolIDE: 4.6.2
- Python: 3.4.3 (32bits)
- Qt: 5.4.1
- PyQt: 5.4.1
- pyqode.core: 2.7.0.dev2
- pyqode.cobol: 2.6.1
- pyqode.qt: 2.6.0
- pygments: 2.0.2

issue_title: Update modules to take disable=(True/False) instead of overloading value
issue_body:
Instead of setting value=None to negate, use a disable keyword argument.
This may involve updating ansible-eos modules as well.

issue_title: Verilog parser won't detect an instance within generate
issue_body:
The following is not detected correctly by `verilog_parser.py`:

```
module name;
genvar i;
  generate
    for( i=0; i < 10; i = i + 1 ) 
      begin: INST_GEN
        true1 instance_name1();
    end
  endgenerate
endmodule
```
It takes `INST_GEN` instead of `true1`.

A unit test to demonstrate this can be found [here](https://github.com/feddischson/vunit/commit/533818b6c37df97a1aa59f3519cf93602b8e046f ). I further added a pull-request for this unit-test.

issue_title: Fix regression for zero values
issue_body:
If zero values are provided for `--newer-than` or `--older-than`, they are currently caught by a [boolean check](https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/0466e4c7c190ca750e69e2c0aef444f0664cbe64/curator/cli/utils.py#L98).  Since zero values are acceptable, this needs to be addressed.

This issue was first reported at https://github.com/elastic/curator/issues/208#issuecomment-84575215

issue_title: put_template() is not working as expected
issue_body:
Hello,

Following the official documentation on [indice template](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/indices-templates.html) I wanted to use the method `put_template` to add an index template. But the method constructs the API call to `PUT /_search/template/{id_of_template}` [code here](https://github.com/elastic/elasticsearch-py/blob/master/elasticsearch/client/__init__.py#L1323) which doesn't seem to work on elasticsearch 6
I corrected the code with the correct API call and it's successfully creating a template index : 
```python
return self.transport.perform_request('PUT', _make_path('_template', id), params=params, body=body)
```
Was I right to correct the method or should I use another method to create index template ?

Best regards

issue_title: Increase _MAX_BLOB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES?
issue_body: Hi @alfpark I'm back trying again to upload some >200GB blobs since the [size limitation was greatly increased late last year](https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/blog/general-availability-larger-block-blobs-in-azure-storage/). As I understand that article, the maximum chunk/block size is increased from 4MB to 100MB. In blobxfer.py we still seem to have hardcoded `_MAX_BLOB_CHUNK_SIZE_BYTES = 4194304`, and the 50,000 chunk limit, so it won't initiate an upload above the old 195GB limit. Should that constant be increased?

issue_title: API calls for template progress are too frequent
issue_body:
### Description
**Outline the issue here:**

API calls to the monitor API happen every second which means I'm more likely to hit my quota.
This should be reduced to say 10 seconds.

Now that the API calls only happen when I specify --verbose (or have file logging enabled) it's not a major issue but still potentially something to be addressed as users can run into it.

cc: @oakeyc @johanste

issue_title: [Storage] Could the create command be simplified?
issue_body: It would be great if the `az storage account create` command didn't require a location parameter, and instead defaulted it from the specified resource group. Additionally, could the `--sku` parameter have a default value? Other services have default values for their SKU (e.g. `az appservice plan create`) and it definitely helps the getting started experience a bit when you're just looking to explore/evaluate something.

az: error: argument _command_package: invalid choice: appservices

issue_title: [KeyVault] Get-default-policy returns gibberish in Python2
[Expanded params] Should be an option to load file contents as a list
issue_body: Today we have @file to load contents from a file (loads it as one chunk, useful for SSH keys, etc.).  We should also have an option to load contents as a list, say with #file.  This would be especially useful in Windows where shell substitution isn't supported.  Windows users could then pipe IDs to a file and execute a command like az vm show --ids #file

issue_title: Confusing/repetitive error message for invalid commands
issue_body:
Take a look at

```
# az foo
usage: az [-h] [--output {json,tsv,list}] [--verbose] [--debug]
          [--query JMESPATH]
          {storage,resource,network,role,redis,login,vmss,vm,ad,tag,webapp,account,logout,component,feedback,taskhelp}
          ...

issue_title: proxy behavior is inconsistent
issue_body:
## Long story short

request proxy behavior is inconsistent within one session:

```Python
import asyncio

import aiohttp


class ProxyCrawler:
    def __init__(self):
        loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
        self.session = aiohttp.ClientSession(loop=loop)

    async def req(self):
        async with self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/ip') as resp:
            text = await resp.text()
            print('First request:')
            print(text)

        async with self.session.get('http://httpbin.org/ip', proxy='http://127.0.0.1:1087') as resp:
            text = await resp.text()
            print('Second request:')
            print(text)

        await self.session.close()


if __name__ == '__main__':
    loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
    c = ProxyCrawler()
    loop.run_until_complete(c.req())

```

## Expected behaviour

The first request goes directly, and the second request goes through the specified proxy.

## Actual behaviour

Both requests go directly without proxy.

( But if you set the first request go through a proxy, the second request will go through the specified proxy correctly. )

## Steps to reproduce

Run the code above ( replace the proxy with yours).

## Your environment

aiohttp: 3.2.0
Mac OS X

issue_title: Multiple workers: seperate package or included in aiohttp
issue_body:
## Background

gunicorn, tornado, sanic & japronto all use the same system of multiple processes connecting to the same socket to utilise multiple CPUs. Unlike tornado sanic and japronto; aiohttp does not have built-in support for multiple workers, instead relying on gunicorn or a proper reverse proxy like nginx, this must have been a conscious decision but I can't find it documented anywhere.

## Question

If I want this functionality should I submit a PR to include it in aiohttp or create a new package?

## Why not gunicorn?

Gunicorn has been a trusted friend for many years, and I'm using it in production on numerous projects. For projects using uwsgi it's invaluable.

However, it's a victim of its own success/age and much of it's API is far out of sync with python 3.5/6. It's also pretty big and unwieldy as it was designed for wsgi and to do lots more than just connect multiple processes with to the same socket and supervise them.

Also as the docs say:

> But nothing is for free: running aiohttp application under gunicorn is slightly slower.

## The Replacement

Whether the replacement for gunicorn is builtin or a separate package it can be extremely concise and easy to reason with:

```python
from multiprocessing import Process
from socket import SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, socket
from aiohttp import web

def serve_multiple(app, workers):
    sock = socket()
    sock.setsockopt(SOL_SOCKET, SO_REUSEADDR, 1)
    sock.bind(('0.0.0.0', 8000))
    sock.set_inheritable(True)

    processes = []
    for i in range(workers):
        process = Process(target=web.run_app, name=f'worker-{i}', kwargs=dict(app=app, sock=sock))
        process.daemon = True
        process.start()
        processes.append(process)

    try:
        for process in processes:
            process.join()
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        pass
    finally:
        for process in processes:
            process.terminate()
        sock.close()

async def index(request):
    return web.Response(body='hello world')

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/', index)
serve_multiple(app, 4)
```

(this works and gives almost 4 times the request throughput of just `run_app`).

The only thinks which would need to be added for production (AFAIK) are:
* logging
* restarting workers on failure
* request time limits, this could be implemented as aiohttp middleware
* an equivalent of gunicorn's `max_requests` which gracefully kills that worker, then restarts it
* anything else?

But it appears that aiohttp passes basic authentication headers after redirection to a location defined in last response header even if domain doesnt match with the one that did redirection which causes an issue in my case because amazon requires that there should be only one auth method (in my cause token was in the url for redirection)

once lib follows redirection and domain doesnt match with previous it should NOT send basic auth headers to this domain

end response from S3 ends with an authentication error since there is an auth token in the URL AND a basic auth headers

I'm not sure on available to everyone server that does a redirection to S3

ubuntu 14/16
aiohttp==1.3.3 (same with git master)

issue_title: Change client tracing signatures
issue_body:
Now tracing signal signatures look like 
```
async def on_request_start(session, trace_config_ctx, method, host, port, headers): ...
async def on_send_connection_queued_start(session, trace_config_ctx): ...
```

The problem is the approach is has no space for future extensions.
Most likely we will want to add a connection info to `on_send_connection_queued_start` but it is impossible to do without breaking all existing signal handlers: sending additional `connection` parameter cannot be accepted.

Sure, we can enforce users to write handlers with mandatory `*args` and `**kwargs` like
```
async def on_send_connection_queued_start(session, trace_config_ctx, *args, **kwargs): ...
```
but I expect that users will constantly forgot these extra arguments.

As a solution we can change signal signature for all signals to `async def on_sig(session, ctx, data): ...` where `ctx` is `trace_config_ctx` and `data` is a structure with all signal parameters: url, host, port, headers, connection etc.
It allows to add new param to data in backward compatible manner.

Next question is the `data` type.
I see several options:
1. Plain dict with keys like `host` and `connection`. It works but looks too old fashion, everybody prefer attributes to string keys.
2. [SimpleNamespace](https://docs.python.org/3/library/types.html?highlight=simplenamespace#types.SimpleNamespace). `data.url` looks better than `data['url']`
3. Custom data classes. Honestly it is even better than `SimpleNamespace` at least from documentation perspective: we will need to document every used data structure explicitly.
4. [attrs](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/attrs) classes. The library brings extra dependency (I don't care, aiohttp has several deps already) but provides neat helpers. Using data classes from Python 3.7 is even better but we should support Python 3.5/3.6 for long time.

`Trace` class already has explicit methods for sending every signal, constructing a data class on call is easy and straightforward.

@pfreixes and others, what do you think about?

issue_title: [client] 301 in POST gets redirected to GET
issue_body:
## Long story short

Using `aiohttp-client` to make a `POST` request, if the server responds with a `301` the client makes a new request to the provided URI but the new request is an empty `GET`.

## Expected behaviour

According to [`RFC2616-sec10.3.2`](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.3.2) the method should not change. Emphasis on:

> When automatically redirecting a POST request after
      receiving a 301 status code, some existing HTTP/1.0 user agents
      will **erroneously** change it into a GET request.

## Actual behaviour

According to [`RFC1945-sec9.3`](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc1945#section-9.3), [`RFC2068-sec10.3`](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2068#section-10.3) and [`RFC2616-sec10.3`](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec10.html#sec10.3):

> The action required MAY be carried out by the user agent without interaction with the user if and only if the method used in the second request is GET or HEAD

Now, considering the in-code nature of `aiohttp-client` (i.e. it's not exactly an end-user user-agent) and the fact that `aiohttp-client` provides an option to not follow redirects, one could argue that the above should not strictly apply.

So, the way I see it there are two choices:

- Maintain the HTTP method when redirecting (this is what happens for example when `aiohttp-client` gets a `308` during a `POST` request)

- Do not redirect on any method other that `GET` and `HEAD` (event if follow redirects is `True`)

## Steps to reproduce

Using the following server - client pair

```py
import asyncio

from aiohttp import web

async def rt(request):
    return web.HTTPOk()

@web.middleware
async def print_request(request, handler):

    print(request)
    return (await handler(request))

async def get_app():
    middlewares = [print_request, web.web_middlewares.normalize_path_middleware()]
    app = web.Application(middlewares=middlewares)
    app.router.add_route('POST', '/rt/', rt)
    return app

web.run_app(get_app())
```

```py
import aiohttp
import asyncio

async def pt():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
        async with session.post('http://localhost:8080/rt', data=b'data') as resp:
            print(resp.status)

asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(pt())
```

The client will print:

```
405
```

Indicating a Method Not Allowed (since it used GET on the resource)

and the server will print:

```
<Request POST /rt >                                 
<Request GET /rt/ >
```

Indicating that after the first `POST` on the resource the client tried to `GET` the resource returned by the server.

## Your environment

Ubuntu 17.10
aiohttp 3.3.1

issue_title: Missing DialogStateOut.supplemental_display_text GRPC messages
issue_body:
Hi,

I've written my own client for the GRPC service where I am submitting text based requests. I am receiving GRPC messages containing audio and supplemental_display_text but I only ever receive one supplemental_display_text message per response. This is generally ok and provides a transcript of the assistant response but there are some queries which result in the single supplemental_display_text only containing the last part of the response. e.g.
If you ask the Google Assistant App on iOS the Question "What do you think of Alexa?" one of the available responses is split over 2 speech bubbles as below:-

Q> What to you think of Alexa?
A> Alexa has such a soothing voice
A> I like it

Running the same question and response through the GRPC service you receive the full audio response but only a single supplemental_display_text message just containing the second response "I like it", where as I would expect 2 messages one containing "Alexa has such a soothing voice" and a second containing "I like it", or a single message containing a concatenated response

issue_title: default validation context should add a oo item
issue_body:
```python
from schematics.models import Model
from schematics.types import IntType, StringType
from schematics.types.compound import ModelType


class SomeModel(Model):
    intt = IntType(required=True)
    strt = StringType(required=True)
    
ModelType(SomeModel, required=True).validate({"intt":1, "strt": "abcd"})

```

See python code above. It would raise exception as following:
```python
.../schematics/types/compound.pyc in _convert(self, value, context)
    124             raise ConversionError(
    125                 "Input must be a mapping or '%s' instance" % self.model_class.__name__)
--> 126         if context.convert and context.oo:
    127             return model_class(value, context=context)
    128         else:

AttributeError: 'Context' object has no attribute 'oo'
```
After tracing back, it seems the default validation context misses an item "oo".

```python
.../schematics/validate.py

def get_validation_context(**options):
    validation_options = {
        'field_converter': validation_converter,
        'partial': False,
        'strict': False,
        'convert': True,
        'validate': True,
        'new': False,
--->    'oo': False
    }
    validation_options.update(options)
    return Context(**validation_options)
```

Inserting the line above solves the problem.

issue_title: xdist bails out too soon when using --exitfirst
issue_body:
While investigating pytest-dev/pytest#442 I ran into an issue where xdist behaves differently than regular pytest.

Basically, it seems that when using `--exitfirst` or `--maxfail=X`, in combination with `-n X`,  pytest (`DSession`?) bails out before collecting the teardown report, so the `pytest_runtest_logreport` doesn't get run.

At first glance, it seems like the the plugin should flush any unread entries from the queue after telling the nodes to stop.

I'm willing to work on this if someone can verify that this makes sense and/or point me in the right direction :)

issue_title: Override offers.Benefits and change TYPE_CHOICES
issue_body:
Is there a conventional way to tackle this? My override model looks like:

```
class Benefit(AbstractBenefit):
    """
    Override Benefit model so we can inject our own Discount choice and model
    """

    # Benefit types
    MEMBERSHIP_DISCOUNT = (
        "Membership Discount")
    TYPE_CHOICES = (
        (MEMBERSHIP_DISCOUNT, ugettext("Discount is as specified by the product's merchant membership settings")),
    )
```

The problem is that setting the TYPE_CHOICES on the subclass is too late.

I can't override the `type` field directly lest I run into `django.core.exceptions.FieldError: Local field 'type' in class 'Benefit' clashes with field of similar name from base class 'AbstractBenefit'`

I've also tried the following solutions to no effect:

```
    def __init__(self, args, **kwargs):
        type_field = self._meta.get_field_by_name("type")[0]
        type_field.choices = self.TYPE_CHOICES
        super(Benefit, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)
```

and

```
class Benefit:
   ...
Benefit._meta.get_field('type').choices = Benefit.TYPE_CHOICES
```

But both produce errors and I get the feeling they aren't the right solution here.

The `generate_and_write_XXX_keypair()` functions should prompt for a password confirmation.  The `confirm` flag is incorrectly set to False.  See https://github.com/secure-systems-lab/securesystemslib/blob/07bf160803285c449e9f75e519aad588223b2954/securesystemslib/interface.py#L175-L177.

Enter a password for the RSA key:
Confirm:

issue_title: consume(timeout=1) takes 5 seconds
issue_body:
Description
===========
`consume(num_messages=100, timeout=1)` takes about 5 seconds.


How to reproduce
================

consumer.py
```py
import time
from confluent_kafka import Consumer

consumer = Consumer({
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost',
    'group.id': 'example_consumer',
    'default.topic.config': {
        'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'
     }
})

consumer.subscribe(['test_q'])

while True:
    while True:
        print('.')
        batch = consumer.consume(num_messages=100, timeout=1)
        if len(batch)>0:
            break
    print(len(batch))
    print('---')
    time.sleep(1)
```

producer.py
```py
import time
from confluent_kafka import Producer

producer = Producer({
    'bootstrap.servers': 'localhost',
    'queue.buffering.max.messages': 10000000,  # 1e7
})

i = 0

while True:
    i += 1
    print(i)

    msg = 'msg ' + str(i)
    producer.poll(0)
    producer.produce('test_q', msg.encode(), callback=None)
    time.sleep(0.1)
```

open 3 terminals and run

1. `python consumer.py`
2. `python producer.py`
3. `./bin/kafka-console-consumer.sh --zookeeper localhost --topic test_q`

You can see the immediate consumption in the terminal3.
The producer sends 10 messages/sec so I expect the consumer to return the batch of size 10.

However, terminal1 prints every 5 seconds. consumer.consume doesn't return for 5 seconds.



Checklist
=========
Please provide the following information:

 - [ ] confluent-kafka-python and librdkafka version (`confluent_kafka.version()` and `confluent_kafka.libversion()`): ('0.11.4', 721920), ('0.11.4', 722175)
 - [ ] Apache Kafka broker version: 2.12-1.1.0
 - [ ] Client configuration: `{...}`
 - [ ] Operating system:
 - [ ] Provide client logs (with `'debug': '..'` as necessary)
 - [ ] Provide broker log excerpts
 - [ ] Critical issue

issue_title: tzfile does not correctly parse files due to its ignoring of leap data
issue_body:
The relevant part in the tzfile implementation does not read from the file if leapcnt is given:

https://github.com/dateutil/dateutil/blob/master/dateutil/tz/tz.py#L309-L312:

            # Not used, for now
            # if leapcnt:
            #    leap = struct.unpack(">%dl" % (leapcnt*2),
            #                         fileobj.read(leapcnt*8))

...which is wrong ;-) as you have to read (or seek forward in the file) this data even if you don't intend to use it. I suspect this hasn't been a problem in practice as the following data parts (isstd and isgmt information) basically aren't used anymore, after they've been set as ttinfo attributes.

issue_title: Implement persons pages
Restructure application
issue_body:
Restructure codebase to move towards a true [Django reusable app](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/intro/reusable-apps/). I'll be handling this over the next couple weeks, but wanted to make an issue to keep everybody in the loop. The goal of this restructure is to make Dispatch easier to distribute outside of The Ubyssey. It will mostly be moving files around and won't require much code change beyond renaming import statements.

- Change `/apps` to `/modules`
- Move `/apps/api` to `/api/v1`
- Rename `/modules/core` to `/module/auth`
- Create a new `/dispatch/models.py` file that imports models from all modules
- Get rid of `/conf/default_settings.py`, find a better solution for defining default settings
- Move `/dispatch/static/manager` to a separate `dispatch-manager` repo

After this is complete, users will be able to create an empty Django project and simply include Dispatch in their `INSTALLED_APPS` setting:

```py
# some-project/settings.py

INSTALLED_APPS = (
  'dispatch',
  # ...
)

# Single dictionary to hold all Dispatch settings
DISPATCH = {
  'enable_api': True,
  'admin_url': '/admin'
  # ...
}

issue_title: Refactor the search feature
issue_body:
## Description: 

Currently, we sync via RPC to a local "contractdb" for performing search operations. This method is inefficient and has a lot of other problems, including inaccurate reflection of account states (balances), and more.

There is already [support for searching there state trie of a local geth node](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/tree/master/mythril/leveldb). However, the way it is currently implemented, it only works for small testnets. The [EthLevelDB class](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/blob/master/mythril/leveldb/client.py) first parses the complete state to create a list of all 'active' contracts in memory before search operations can be invoked. Obviously this is infeasible if the state trie is large.

To make this usable, it needs to be implemented in a way that allows users to iterate through contracts in the state trie either one-by-one, or by providing a search expression that invokes a callback. 

## Implementation details:Initial implementation ideas/instruction

1. Refactor the LevelDB client class to allow one to iterate over contracts in the geth leveldb instead of reading all contracts into memory in advance.

2. Refactor the search feature to invoke the new leveldb client class. Remove all references to contract storage.

3. Remove the old persistence layer ([contractstorage.py](https://github.com/ConsenSys/mythril/blob/master/mythril/ether/contractstorage.py)), the ZODB requirement and the `--init-db` feature. 

Once the implementation is done, search only works if a validlocal geth leveldb exists. Otherwise, the user is shown an error message.

issue_title: JSONRenderer modifies data when status_code is `204 NO CONTENT`
issue_body:
`JSONRenderer` modifies the data to be renedered, from `None` to `{'data': None}` when `response.status_code` is 204.

From [HTTP's RFC](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-4.3) I see that:
> All responses to the HEAD request method
   MUST NOT include a message-body, even though the presence of entity-
   header fields might lead one to believe they do. All 1xx
   (informational), 204 (no content), and 304 (not modified) responses
   MUST NOT include a message-body

This behavior made some clients to crash when the response stauts is `204 NO CONTENT` and the header `Content-Length` is different from 0 (in this case 13 since the response body is `{"data":null}`).

issue_title: ffi.cdef parsing causes build failure on PyPy 5.8 virtualenv
issue_body:
## Environment
PyPy 5.8 on virtualenv
Using the latest commit `a87324f`

## Description
PyPy 5.8 uses different error message format for cffi.CDefError to Python 2.7.13, which causes build failure.

Python 2.7.13 error format example
```
cannot parse "x &= 0xFFFFFFFFLL;"
:34:6: before: &=
```

PyPy 5.8 error format example
```
cannot parse "Int r = (x == 0) ? ((Bool)0) : ((Bool)1);"
<cdef source string>:31:30: before: 0
```
```
<cdef source string>:32: unexpected <FuncDef>: this construct is valid C but not valid in cdef()
```

The second error format causes out of bound exception, because it has two `:` but only one line.
https://github.com/angr/pyvex/blob/a87324f88aa621436d4b8077b828b92ce51e08c2/make_ffi.py#L41-L42

I think it would be nice to have a mark/flag (watched) videos option in the saved playlists indicated by a asterisk character between the _Number_ and the _Title_: 

**Examples:**

```
Num    Title                     Time

issue_title: Race condition in monitoring when copying jobs
issue_body:
In a clean Ganga session with a completely wiped `~/ganga_dir`, running the following:

```python
j = Job(); j.submit(); j.copy(); j.copy()
```

causes an exception in the monitoring loop about 80% of the time. The exception given is:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Lib/Job/Job.py", line 611, in updateStatus
    self.time.timenow(str(newstatus))
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Lib/Job/JobTime.py", line 127, in timenow
    be_statetime = j.backend.getStateTime(childstatus)
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/Lib/Localhost/Localhost.py", line 112, in getStateTime
    j = self.getJobObject()
  File ".../ganga/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Base/Objects.py", line 1025, in getJobObject
    raise AssertionError('no job associated with object ' + repr(self))
AssertionError: no job associated with object <Ganga.Lib.Localhost.Localhost.Localhost object at 0x314c650>
```

Pausing for a second between after the `submit()` operation makes the race condition disappear:

```python
from time import sleep
j = Job(); j.submit(); sleep(1); j.copy(); j.copy()
```

This came up when converting the JIRA1961 test to the new system.

issue_title: Flushing a server breaks migration forever
issue_body:
Using the Kinto `flush` plugin wipes the metadata table, which removes information about the storage schema version. This means it is impossible to do a migration, because we no longer have any idea what version to migrate *from*.

There is currently error-handling logic which tries to accommodate for this. It assumes that 1. if the metadata table is empty, then the server was flushed, and 2. if the server was flushed, the schema version in-place is the "current" maximal one. Point 2 is obviously wrong in the case of e.g. local development, where you might flush the server and then, much later, try to build and run a new Kinto version. Point 1 is about to become wrong as we add migration to the permission backend, which may store data in the same metadata table. On top of all of this, the code is not clearly marked as error-handling logic, although it really is.

issue_title: Use bipartite graph matching algorithm for alldifferent global constraint
issue_body:
Are there any plans to improve the performance of the alldifferent global constraint?
The current approach checks feasibility well, but doesn't contribute to pruning.
You can get a lot of pruning done using this constraint by using an algorithm that involves finding the maximum matching of a bipartite graph (graph where variables and values are the bipartite vertices and edges are between the variables and values in their domains).

Check it out https://www.andrew.cmu.edu/user/vanhoeve/papers/alldiff.pdf

issue_title: Instantiate graphql types with _ attributes
issue_body:
Given a type e.g.

```
class Some(graphene.ObjectType):
  _state = None // some state I do not want to have in the schema
 _other_state = None
```

I have to instantiate this as follows which is quite cumbersome and feels very unpythonic:
```
s = Some()
s._state = 'secret'
s._other_state = 'other_secret'
```
where-as it would be nice to be able to just do `s = Some(_state='secret', _other_state='other_secret')`. I know you can do `s = Some(_root={'_state': 'secret', '_other_state': 'other_secret'})` but that makes it far less explicit about what state I am sharing and especially in a big schema this can turn into a problem quickly as it is very easy to loose track of what is shared with what type.

So it would be nice if we could get the best of both worlds and just allow `s = Some(_state='secret', _other_state='other_secret')

issue_title: Stack-Policies should be applied before stacks are applied if Stack already exists
issue_body:
If a stack is already existing and you add a Stack Policy to it, this policy is not added until the stack sync has finished. This means that when you do the sync with the stack policy, it will still be possible to do changes that are forbidden by the stack policy. 

It also means that if you temporarily want to change a policy to make it more allowing to be able to do a change, this won't be possible because the stack-sync will fail and the policy will never be updated.

Desired behavior would be that if the stack is already existing in the account, it should apply the policy first, if not it should first create the stack and then apply the policy.

issue_title: examples/posix/flash.c problems with self-checks.
issue_body:
Technically there is nothing wrong with this, but depending upon the machine validation check-tests run on, the tests fail.  The current result, depending on the environment where the self-test operations are run they fail - randomly, but run perfectly when the next time.

The intent here is to fix/address the problem so that the validation tests work on more machines.

During the FLASH.C init() call, the simulated flash is erased - the current configuration is a simulated 256K byte flash chip (page size: 0x800(2K), npages=128 = 256K) 

Underlying problem:  The ${openthread}/tests/scripts/thread-cert directory - is run with numerous test scripts in parallel.  For example  Cert_5_2_06_RouterDowngrade.py - launches 26 instances of the CLI, Cert-5_2_03_LeaderReject2Hops.py launches 33 instances - the point here is there is a huge number of instances of the CLI application running in parallel.

The above to factors together cause the errors.

Solution/Option that is rejected:  Changing theses tests to run in *SEQUENCE* (not parallel) takes, means some tests take more then 3 hours to complete on the Travis-CI servers, well beyond a reasonable time limit. In fact, Travis dies with timeout errors.

Solution/Option that is rejected: Adjusting the total number of parallel jobs may solve the solution for one environment, but not all test environments, and extend test time on fast machines.

Solution/Option that is rejected: Just use a Bigger/Faster machines, this solution may work on sever class machine, but it is better if all developers can run the complete test suite on their assigned laptop, which may for example actually use a virtual machine. The developer tells the boss: I need a new laptop, the biggest, the baddest, the fastest thing on the planet.  The Boss says: What are you smoking?

Problem Detail 1 - The current implementation of page erase writes single bytes by calling pwrite()

Thus there are 50 instances of the CLI app - making 256K calls to the pwrite() function, each writing a single byte - depending upon the load on the machine running the test  (typically a VM of some sort) things timeout and fail for random reasons.

The solution is in this function *write* full pages in one operation, obviously (PAGE SIZE) 2048 times faster.

Problem Detail 2 - which is more of a comment of how *NOT* to fix this, and not cause other problems.  I learned this during my attempt to resolve the above issue.

To accomplish 1 (full page writes) - we need a PAGESIZE array of bytes, this could be: STATIC allocation,  MALLOC allocation, or on the runtime stack.

Static is not always a good solution.

The way to *NOT* solve this is to use MALLOC, meaning:  Allocate a page, initialize the page to 0xff, write the page, then free the page.

here's why:  Some (not all) of the tests make use of the address sanitizer  (aka: libasan, or just ASAN) the address sanitizer has a feature to detect  use-memory-after-free. To accomplish this, the function free() is changed to *NEVER* release the memory, instead free() "poisons" the freed memory in some form but does not release the memory. The net effect, the ASAN feature becomes a rather massively large memory leak.

Part 1: Thus any application that makes *MANY* malloc/free calls will cause a huge pseudo-memory leak. The CLI applications do exactly this as part of the flash page erase operation.

Part 2: Because the CERT tests execute well more then 50 tests in parallel, each with their own "pseudo-massive-memory-leak" means the test machine requires a HUGE amount of RAM (or swap space) this, coupled with virtual memory paging often results in either (a) timeout failures, (b) random strange crashes in Python due to out of memory errors, or (c) random failures in the ASAN enabled tests due to allocation failures.

On travis-ci, if you run the test second, or third time it sometimes passes, but another combination of tests fail, what is generally consistent is exactly an "out-of-memory" error/failure due to malloc/free + ASAN.

The simple solution is, in this case to place the dummy page on the runtime stack,  and not use malloc/free, and not use static memory allocation.

issue_title: Should I add '<' and '>' to get char ngram vectors?
issue_body:
According to [Enriching Word Vectors with Subword Information](https://arxiv.org/abs/1607.04606), FastText adds special boundary symbols < and > at the beginning and end of words, allowing to distinguish prefixes and suffixes from other character sequences. 

For example, "where" is represented as <wh, whe, her, ere, re>, and <where>, if n=3. 

What I want to do is to use fastText to get vectors for individual character ngram (not for the entire word). For example, I expect to get 6 vectors for "where", which will be used to do initialization. These vectors will be continued to train afterwards. I download the English fastText from [fasttext.cc](https://fasttext.cc/docs/en/pretrained-vectors.html), which is trained based on wiki.

My questions: Does fastText literaly store '<' and '>' in the vocabulary? Should I pass a char ngram with '<' and/or '>' to get the vector OR without them?

For instance, for 'where':
```
en_model = FastText.load_fasttext_format('../FastText/wiki.en/wiki.en')
# OPTION 1:
word_vector = []
for charngram in ['wh', 'whe', 'her', 'ere', 're', 'where']:
    word_vector.append(en_model[charngram])
# OPTION 2:
word_vector = []
for charngram in ['<wh', 'whe', 'her', 'ere', 're>', '<where>']:
    word_vector.append(en_model[charngram])
```

issue_title: Dropping support for Pythons in fetch.py
issue_body: If we drop support for a version of Python previously supported by fetch.py, existing certbot-auto installations can fail. If we're unable to reboostrap because we don't have the user's permission, when certbot-auto is run again during phase 1 it will call `DeterminePythonVersion` and if we can't find an appropriate Python, we exit with an error. Instead, we should print a message about being unable to check for upgrades.

issue_title: clean measurement and tag values for stats
issue_body:
According to https://docs.influxdata.com/influxdb/v1.2/write_protocols/line_protocol_tutorial/
we have to escape ` `, `,`, `=`, `"`. 
I noticed this when a funcserver was having a name with spaces. Influxdb was rejecting the line protocol string.
Instead of escaping them with backslashes, I've decided to remove them if they are present.

issue_title: Investigate documentation checking
issue_body:
One big goal is to analyse docstrings that students write for their modules, classes, and functions. However, it seems like the AST parsing that `ast` and `astroid` perform do not expose docstrings, so we can't access them through the AST. (Am I wrong? Would be nice to find out.)

However, pylint does seem to check something about docstrings (e.g., there one error it checks is "missing docstring"). How does it do it, and how can we build on it?

Probably good to investigate the `DocstringChecker` class defined here:
https://github.com/PyCQA/pylint/blob/master/pylint/checkers/base.py#L1319

@WChrisK I'm giving you this one since it may or may not be a bit involved.

issue_title: Request should present a scope-like interface
issue_body: The `Request` class should present a dict-like interface so that it can be used in the same way as `scope`. Should also allow it to be instantiated without a `receive` channel being set initially.

issue_title: Replace youckan by the builtin auth
issue_body:
Youckan is present for historical reasons, when Udata had no builtin auth system and multiple Etalab apps had to connect to an SSO.

These apps were removed and since then udata has a builtin login system with SSO capabilities.

We may therefore consider removing Youckan, canceling id.data.gouv.fr development and promote the usage of the internal login system.

issue_title: Container interface improvements
issue_body:
Separated from an earlier offline discussion: I think perhaps 

```
[global]
parameter: container_type = "docker"

[default]
bash: container = f'{container_type}:gaow/debian-ngs'
```

is more general than the current interface. Reasons: 

1. If we later support singularity we need a unified interface
2. With this, we can have `container_type = "local"` or something like it to disable the use of containers easily for a given run.

issue_title: The Steam backend should use steamid instead of personaname to identify users
issue_body:
[Steam backend](https://github.com/python-social-auth/social-core/blob/master/social_core/backends/steam.py#L27):

The personaname (display name on the profile) ist not unique and can therefore lead to errors, when multiple users with the same name try to log on. The steamid would provide a number as the username but is unique throughout Steam.

issue_title: `interactive_output` flickers
issue_body:
Similar to the problem described [here](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/910), I want to customize the layout of my interactive widgets. The kludgy approach is to call `interactive` then get the children out, put them in a specific layout, and put them back in. Like so:

```
from IPython.display import display
import numpy as np
from ipywidgets import *
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_oscillation(wavelength, magnitude, phase):
    x = np.linspace(-20, 20, 1000)
    plt.plot(x, magnitude*np.sin((x+phase)/(wavelength/(2*np.pi))))
    plt.show()
    
w = dict(wavelength = FloatSlider(2*np.pi, min=0.1, max=20, step=0.1, description='wavelength'),
         magnitude = FloatSlider(1, min=0.1, max=10, step=0.1, description='magnitude'),
         phase = FloatSlider(0, min=0, max=10, step=0.1, description='phase'))

int_w = interactive(plot_oscillation, **w)

output = int_w.children[-1] # last child is the output

int_w.children = [HBox([VBox([*int_w.children[:-1]]), output])]
display(int_w)
```
This approach works and does *not* flicker, but it's pretty clunky. The `interactive_output` function was created to provide a better way to do this sort of thing. It works but flickers. See here:

```
from IPython.display import display
import numpy as np
from ipywidgets import *
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

def plot_oscillation(wavelength, magnitude, phase):
    x = np.linspace(-20, 20, 1000)
    plt.plot(x, magnitude*np.sin((x+phase)/(wavelength/(2*np.pi))))
    plt.show()
    
w = dict(wavelength = FloatSlider(2*np.pi, min=0.1, max=20, step=0.1, description='wavelength'),
         magnitude = FloatSlider(1, min=0.1, max=10, step=0.1, description='magnitude'),
         phase = FloatSlider(0, min=0, max=10, step=0.1, description='phase'))

output = interactive_output(plot_oscillation, w)
box = HBox([VBox([*w.values()]), output])
display(box)
```
It seems like it might be related to [this](https://github.com/jupyter-widgets/ipywidgets/issues/145) now-fixed issue with `interact`.

issue_title: Method for apps to determine their target site
issue_body:
Currently we don't have a method to indicate to the app the site it has been assigned to. If we do this with env variables it will be consistent across both `python` and `bash` apps. It would be simple to have this be set via the worker env.
Alternatively we could just push out a solution for #40 and reuse the same methods for site specific env variables as well.

I believe the data management ( #9, #71, #38 ) issues are dependent on this.

issue_title: Make `default_dataloader` a property and remove with_dataloader argument
issue_body:
As noted in https://github.com/kipoi/kipoi/issues/321, it's counter-intuitive that the dataloader is automatically loaded with the model.

`with_dataloader`  in 
```python
def get_model(model, source="kipoi", with_dataloader=True):
```
should be removed and `defatul_dataloader` should be a property, lazily evaluated.

issue_title: Indexing takes too long for 1M 128 dimensional arrays
issue_body:
First of all thanks for this awesome effort.

I'm trying to test NMSLib with large 128 dimensional numpy arrays but my code seems to take a long time to index (More than 50 seconds).

Are my parameters unoptimized? I want to aim for more than 10M datapoints but I fear the indexing time grows exponentially.

```
import time

import nmslib
import numpy as np

NUMBER_OF_SAMPLES = 1000000
NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS = 128

start_time = time.time()
array = np.random.random_sample((NUMBER_OF_SAMPLES, NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS)).astype(np.float32)
print("Finished generating the data => %s seconds" % np.mean(time.time() - start_time))

M = 10
efC = 20
efS = 10
num_nbrs = 5
num_threads = 4
post = 0  # Build the Tree twice but gain accuracy
index_time_params = {'M': M, 'indexThreadQty': num_threads, 'efConstruction': efC, 'post': post}
print('Index-time parameters', index_time_params)

start_time = time.time()
nmslib_index = nmslib.init(method='hnsw', space='cosinesimil')
nmslib_index.addDataPointBatch(array)
nmslib_index.createIndex(index_time_params, print_progress=True)
nmslib_index.setQueryTimeParams({'efSearch': efS})
print("\nTree Initialization Time => %s" % np.mean(time.time() - start_time))
array = None

for i in range(0, 10):
    ids, distances = nmslib_index.knnQuery(np.random.rand(1, NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS), k=num_nbrs)

times = []
for i in range(0, 100):
    start_time = time.time()
    ids, distances = nmslib_index.knnQuery(np.random.rand(1, NUMBER_OF_DIMENSIONS), k=5)
    times.append(time.time() - start_time)
print("Average Search Time => %s" % np.mean(times))
```
